{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Rokko Hime Daizen Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= [[File:Rokkohime shrine, Kobe.jpg|280px|The inner shrine of Tamonji, Rokko Hime Shrine]] |location= {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}}, {{ill|Kobe|ja|神戸市}}, {{ill|Nada Ward|ja|灘区}}, Rokkōyama Town |Latitude degrees = 34 | Latitude minutes = 45 | Latitude seconds = 58.72 |Longitude degrees = 135 | Longitude minutes = 14 | Longitude seconds = 21.91 |Map= Kobe#Japan Hyogo |deity= Rokko Hime Daizen Kami ({{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime}}) |Sacred object= {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座}} |Shrine rank= |Established= |Main hall= |Other names= |Pilgrimage sites= |festival= |Rituals= }} '''Rokko Hime Daizen Shrine''' (六甲比命大善神社, Rokkō Hime Daizen Jinja) is a Shinto shrine located in Rokkōyama Town, {{ill|Nada Ward|ja|灘区}}, {{ill|Kobe|ja|神戸市}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}}. It enshrines an awe-inspiring {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座}} (sacred rock formation) as its sacred object.
Situated near the ridge of Mount Rokkō, the shrine is close to another sacred rock associated with {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|天穂日命}}, located within the Rokkō Mountain Country House grounds.
The main sacred object, a grand Iwakura known as Yōga Iwa, is nestled between the areas of Un-ga Iwa and Shinkyō Iwa. The shrine serves as the inner sanctuary of {{ill|Tamonji|ja|多聞寺}}, a temple founded in the 7th century by the Indian ascetic {{ill|Hōdō Sennin|ja|法道仙人}} in the Karato district of northern Kobe.<ref>Makoto Tanabe, *Legends of Kobe*, Kobe Shimbun General Publishing Center.</ref>
== deity == * Rokko Hime Daizen Kami ({{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天}}/{{ill|Kichijōten|ja|吉祥天}}), also identified with Tsu Kisakaki Itsu-no-mitama Amasakaru Mukatsu-hime ({{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime}}).
The name of the enshrined deity has evolved alongside changes in the naming of Mount Rokkō. As a water deity, the kami's virtues are prominently evident in the region surrounding Mount Rokkō, including Japan's three famous hot springs (Arima Onsen), the renowned Nunobiki Falls (source of Kobe Water), and the legendary Miyamizu water of Nada Gō.
Historically, Mount Rokkō was referred to by the kanji 武庫 (Mukō), pronounced "Mukō-yama" or "Mukō-san," and in earlier times as "Mukatsu-mine," as noted in {{ill|Kamo no Mabuchi|ja|賀茂真淵}}'s *Kanji-kō*. This suggests that the deity was originally known as Mukatsu-hime and later as Mukō-hime before evolving to its current name. The reading "Mukatsu" for "Rokkō" further su11orts this hypothesis.
Mount Rokkō was once part of the shrine territory of {{ill|Hirota Shrine|ja|廣田神社}}.<ref>*Nishinomiya Shrine*, Gakuseisha, p. 53.</ref> The deity enshrined at Hirota Shrine, Tsu Kisakaki Itsu-no-mitama Amasakaru Mukatsu-hime (another name for {{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime}}), shares the name with the ancient Mukatsu-mine, aligning the two sites historically and theologically.
== History == The sacred Iwakura of Rokko Hime Shrine is believed to be a man-made structure constructed by stacking massive stones, dating back to the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}}. It is speculated to have been created around the Middle Jōmon period (circa 3000 BCE).
The shrine was historically associated with the Four Oni family of {{ill|En no Gyōja|ja|役行者}}, who managed the western Rokkō mountains after migrating from the {{ill|Dōsen village|ja|洞川}} in {{ill|Tenkawa Village|ja|天川村}}.
According to the Rokko Hime Association, the shrine once served as an auxiliary shrine to Hirota Shrine and was also linked to the {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮}}, a sub-shrine of the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|lt=inner shrine|ja|皇大神宮}} of {{ill|Ise Jingu|ja|伊勢神宮}}. The association posits that the deity Rokko Hime is synonymous with Seoritsu-hime and attributes the name "Rokkō" to the presence of this sacred site. These interpretations are informed by the ancient texts of {{ill|Hotsuma Tsutae|ja|ホツマツタエ}}.
The shrine is regarded as the primary sanctuary for worshiping Seoritsu-hime.
In the 7th century, the Indian monk {{ill|Hōdō Sennin|ja|法道仙人}} founded {{ill|Tamonji|ja|多聞寺}} on Mount Rokkō. The Buddhist deity {{ill|Bishamonten|ja|毘沙門天}} (Vaiśravaṇa) descended upon the rock formation Un-ga Iwa within the shrine's precincts, establishing its connection to the temple.
During the Edo period, the shrine and the temple were jointly managed. However, with the Meiji government’s {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離}} policy, the sacred sites became administratively separated, though they remain closely associated today.
Currently, the shrine is managed jointly by Tamonji and the Rokko Hime Association. Annual autumn rituals include Goma fire offerings conducted by the temple's head priest and the association.
== Recent Developments == In recent years, Rokko Hime Shrine has gained recognition as a {{ill|Power spot|ja|パワースポット}}, attracting numerous visitors. Various group events are held in front of Shinkyō Iwa. The shrine's offerings include detailed pamphlets, Goshuin stamps, talismans dedicated to Rokko Hime Daizen Kami, charms for Seoritsu-hime, musical water bells, and decorative plaques featuring dragons or Kichijōten.
== Access == * By car: Accessible via the Rokko Mountain Roads from Takarazuka, Nishinomiya, or Ashiya. Visitors can aim for the nearby Rokko Mountain Athletic Park or Snow Park, with parking available near the shrine. * By public transport: From JR Shin-Kobe Station, take the subway to Sannomiya, then transfer to JR Tokaido Line for Rokko Station. From there, the Rokko Cable Car and mountain buses provide access to the shrine.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Rokkohime shrine, Kobe 002.jpg|Winter a11roach to the shrine File:六甲山 心経岩.JPG|Shinkyō Iwa File:六甲比命大善神拝殿 額.JPG|Plaque at the main shrine File:六甲比命大善神の御神体.JPG|Sacred Iwakura of Rokko Hime Daizen Shrine </gallery>

It enshrines an awe-inspiring {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座|12=draft}} (sacred rock formation) as its sacred object.
Situated near the ridge of Mount Rokkō, the shrine is close to another sacred rock associated with {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|天穂日命|12=draft}}, located within the Rokkō Mountain Country House grounds.
The main sacred object, a grand Iwakura known as Yōga Iwa, is nestled between the areas of Un-ga Iwa and Shinkyō Iwa. The shrine serves as the inner sanctuary of {{ill|Tamonji|ja|多聞寺|12=draft}}, a temple founded in the 7th century by the Indian ascetic {{ill|Hōdō Sennin|ja|法道仙人|12=draft}} in the Karato district of northern Kobe.<ref>Makoto Tanabe, *Legends of Kobe*, Kobe Shimbun General Publishing Center.</ref>
== deity == * Rokko Hime Daizen Kami ({{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天|12=draft}}/{{ill|Kichijōten|ja|吉祥天|12=draft}}), also identified with Tsu Kisakaki Itsu-no-mitama Amasakaru Mukatsu-hime ({{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|12=draft}}).
The name of the enshrined deity has evolved alongside changes in the naming of Mount Rokkō. As a water deity, the kami's virtues are prominently evident in the region surrounding Mount Rokkō, including Japan's three famous hot springs (Arima Onsen), the renowned Nunobiki Falls (source of Kobe Water), and the legendary Miyamizu water of Nada Gō.
Historically, Mount Rokkō was referred to by the kanji 武庫 (Mukō), pronounced "Mukō-yama" or "Mukō-san," and in earlier times as "Mukatsu-mine," as noted in {{ill|Kamo no Mabuchi|ja|賀茂真淵|12=draft}}'s *Kanji-kō*. This suggests that the deity was originally known as Mukatsu-hime and later as Mukō-hime before evolving to its current name. The reading "Mukatsu" for "Rokkō" further su11orts this hypothesis.
Mount Rokkō was once part of the shrine territory of {{ill|Hirota Shrine|ja|廣田神社|12=draft}}.<ref>*Nishinomiya Shrine*, Gakuseisha, p. 53.</ref> The deity enshrined at Hirota Shrine, Tsu Kisakaki Itsu-no-mitama Amasakaru Mukatsu-hime (another name for {{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|12=draft}}), shares the name with the ancient Mukatsu-mine, aligning the two sites historically and theologically.
== History == The sacred Iwakura of Rokko Hime Shrine is believed to be a man-made structure constructed by stacking massive stones, dating back to the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代|12=draft}}. It is speculated to have been created around the Middle Jōmon period (circa 3000 BCE).
The shrine was historically associated with the Four Oni family of {{ill|En no Gyōja|ja|役行者|12=draft}}, who managed the western Rokkō mountains after migrating from the {{ill|Dōsen village|ja|洞川|12=draft}} in {{ill|Tenkawa Village|ja|天川村|12=draft}}.
According to the Rokko Hime Association, the shrine once served as an auxiliary shrine to Hirota Shrine and was also linked to the {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|12=draft}}, a sub-shrine of the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|lt=inner shrine|ja|皇大神宮|12=draft}} of {{ill|Ise Jingu|ja|伊勢神宮|12=draft}}. The association posits that the deity Rokko Hime is synonymous with Seoritsu-hime and attributes the name "Rokkō" to the presence of this sacred site. These interpretations are informed by the ancient texts of {{ill|Hotsuma Tsutae|ja|ホツマツタエ|12=draft}}.
The shrine is regarded as the primary sanctuary for worshiping Seoritsu-hime.
In the 7th century, the Indian monk {{ill|Hōdō Sennin|ja|法道仙人|12=draft}} founded {{ill|Tamonji|ja|多聞寺|12=draft}} on Mount Rokkō. The Buddhist deity {{ill|Bishamonten|ja|毘沙門天|12=draft}} (Vaiśravaṇa) descended upon the rock formation Un-ga Iwa within the shrine's precincts, establishing its connection to the temple.
During the Edo period, the shrine and the temple were jointly managed. However, with the Meiji government’s {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離|12=draft}} policy, the sacred sites became administratively separated, though they remain closely associated today.
Currently, the shrine is managed jointly by Tamonji and the Rokko Hime Association. Annual autumn rituals include Goma fire offerings conducted by the temple's head priest and the association.
== Recent Developments == In recent years, Rokko Hime Shrine has gained recognition as a {{ill|Power spot|ja|パワースポット|12=draft}}, attracting numerous visitors. Various group events are held in front of Shinkyō Iwa. The shrine's offerings include detailed pamphlets, Goshuin stamps, talismans dedicated to Rokko Hime Daizen Kami, charms for Seoritsu-hime, musical water bells, and decorative plaques featuring dragons or Kichijōten.
== Access == * By car: Accessible via the Rokko Mountain Roads from Takarazuka, Nishinomiya, or Ashiya. Visitors can aim for the nearby Rokko Mountain Athletic Park or Snow Park, with parking available near the shrine. * By public transport: From JR Shin-Kobe Station, take the subway to Sannomiya, then transfer to JR Tokaido Line for Rokko Station. From there, the Rokko Cable Car and mountain buses provide access to the shrine.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Rokkohime shrine, Kobe 002.jpg|Winter a11roach to the shrine File:六甲山 心経岩.JPG|Shinkyō Iwa File:六甲比命大善神拝殿 額.JPG|Plaque at the main shrine File:六甲比命大善神の御神体.JPG|Sacred Iwakura of Rokko Hime Daizen Shrine </gallery>
== References == <references/>
== See also == * {{ill|Tamonji|ja|多聞寺 (神戸市北区)|12=draft}} * {{ill|Mount Rokko|ja|六甲山|12=draft}} * {{ill|Hirota Shrine|ja|廣田神社|12=draft}} * {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天|12=draft}} * {{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|12=draft}} * {{ill|En no Gyōja|ja|役行者|12=draft}} * {{ill|Rokkō Shugen|ja|六甲修験|12=draft}}

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Rokkō Hime Daizen Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= [[File:Rokkohime shrine, Kobe.jpg|280px|Rokkō Hime Daizen Shrine, Oku-no-in of Taimon-ji Temple]] |location= {{ill|Nada-ku, Kobe|ja|灘区}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}}, {{ill|Rokkōzan Town|ja|六甲山町}} |Latitude = 34 | Latitude_min = 45 | Latitude_sec = 58.72 |Longitude = 135 | Longitude_min = 14 | Longitude_sec = 21.91 |Map= Kobe#Japan Hyogo |deity= Rokkō Hime Daizen God ({{ill|Saeōrihime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime}}) |Sacred_object= {{ill|Iwakura|ja|磐座}} (sacred rock) |Rank= |consecration_year= |Main_hall= |Other_names= |Special_associations= |Festival= |Rituals= }}
'''Rokkō Hime Daizen Shrine''' (六甲比命大善神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Nada-ku, Kobe|ja|灘区}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}}, known for its sacred {{ill|iwakura rock|ja|磐座}} (rock formation) as the object of worship.
Situated near the ridgeline of {{ill|Mount Rokkō|ja|六甲山}}, the shrine is close to other sacred sites such as the rock formation dedicated to {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|天穂日命}} within the Rokkō Country House grounds.
== deity == * Main deity - Rokkō Hime Daizen God (identified as {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天}} and {{ill|Kichijōten|ja|吉祥天}}), also known as Tsukisakaki Itsu-no-Mitama Amasakaru Mukatsu Hime (撞賢木厳魂天疎向津姫, {{ill|Saeōrihime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime}}).
The deity’s name has evolved along with the mountain’s name, reflecting its role as a water god influencing the region’s renowned springs such as {{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉}}, {{ill|Nunobiki Waterfall|ja|布引の滝}}, and the renowned {{ill|Miya Water|ja|宮水}} used in brewing by the Nada Gōgō.
== History == The shrine’s sacred rock formation is believed to be a man-made structure from the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}}, possibly constructed around 5,000 BCE. It was used by early Jōmon people for religious practices.
According to legend, the shrine was once the {{ill|Okunomiya|ja|奥宮}} (inner shrine) of {{ill|Hirota Shrine|ja|廣田神社}} and was also associated with the {{ill|Ara-matsuri-no-miya|ja|荒祭宮}} of the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}.
The current sacred site is managed collaboratively by {{ill|Taimon-ji Temple|ja|多聞寺 (神戸市北区)}} and the {{ill|Rokkō Hime Society|ja|六甲比命講}}, with rituals such as autumn goma fire ceremonies conducted jointly.
== Recent Developments == In recent years, the shrine has gained popularity as a {{ill|power spot|ja|パワースポット}}. Various groups now hold events near the sacred rocks, and visitors can obtain shrine-related materials, such as {{ill|goshuin|ja|御朱印}}, talismans, and photos of sacred imagery.
== Access == === By Car === From {{ill|Takarazuka|ja|宝塚}}, {{ill|Nishinomiya|ja|西宮}}, or {{ill|Kobe|ja|神戸}} via Hyōgo Prefectural Route 16, head toward {{ill|Rokkō Athletic Park GREENIA|ja|六甲山アスレチックパークGREENIA}}. Parking is available near the shrine, with additional paid parking at GREENIA.
=== By Public Transportation === From {{ill|JR Shin-Kobe Station|ja|新神戸駅}}, take the subway to {{ill|Sannomiya|ja|三宮}}. Transfer to JR or Hankyū lines to reach {{ill|Rokkō Station|ja|六甲駅}}. From there, take the bus to Rokkō Cable Car Station, ascend to Rokkōzan Station, and proceed to the shrine.
== Photo Gallery == <gallery> File:六甲比命大善神の御神体.JPG|Sacred rock formation of Rokkō Hime Daizen Shrine File:六甲山 心経岩.JPG|Shingyō Rock File:Rokkohime shrine, Kobe 002.jpg|Approach to the shrine (winter) </gallery>
== References == <references/> == See Also == * {{ill|Taimon-ji Temple|ja|多聞寺 (神戸市北区)}} * {{ill|Mount Rokkō|ja|六甲山}} * {{ill|Hirota Shrine|ja|廣田神社}} * {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天}} * {{ill|Saeōrihime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime}} * {{ill|En no Gyōja|ja|役行者}} * {{ill|Rokkō Shugen|ja|六甲修験}}
== External Links == * [https://rokkouhime.wordpress.com/ Official site of Rokkō Hime Shrine] * [{{NDLDC|864336}} Reference to historical documents]

Visitors can aim for the nearby Rokko Mountain Athletic Park or Snow Park, with parking available near the shrine. * By public transport: From JR Shin-Kobe Station, take the subway to Sannomiya, then transfer to JR Tokaido Line for Rokko Station. From there, the Rokko Cable Car and mountain buses provide access to the shrine.

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Rokkō Hime Daizen Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= [[File:Rokkohime shrine, Kobe.jpg|280px|Rokkō Hime Daizen Shrine, Oku-no-in of Taimon-ji Temple]] |location= {{ill|Nada-ku, Kobe|ja|灘区|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Rokkōzan Town|ja|六甲山町|12=draft}} |Latitude = 34 | Latitude_min = 45 | Latitude_sec = 58.72 |Longitude = 135 | Longitude_min = 14 | Longitude_sec = 21.91 |Map= Kobe#Japan Hyogo |deity= Rokkō Hime Daizen God ({{ill|Saeōrihime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|12=draft}}) |Sacred_object= {{ill|Iwakura|ja|磐座|12=draft}} (sacred rock) |Rank= |consecration_year= |Main_hall= |Other_names= |Special_associations= |Festival= |Rituals= }}
'''Rokkō Hime Daizen Shrine''' (六甲比命大善神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Nada-ku, Kobe|ja|灘区|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県|12=draft}}, known for its sacred {{ill|iwakura rock|ja|磐座|12=draft}} (rock formation) as the object of worship.
Situated near the ridgeline of {{ill|Mount Rokkō|ja|六甲山|12=draft}}, the shrine is close to other sacred sites such as the rock formation dedicated to {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|天穂日命|12=draft}} within the Rokkō Country House grounds.
== deity == * Main deity - Rokkō Hime Daizen God (identified as {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kichijōten|ja|吉祥天|12=draft}}), also known as Tsukisakaki Itsu-no-Mitama Amasakaru Mukatsu Hime (撞賢木厳魂天疎向津姫, {{ill|Saeōrihime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|12=draft}}).
The deity’s name has evolved along with the mountain’s name, reflecting its role as a water god influencing the region’s renowned springs such as {{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nunobiki Waterfall|ja|布引の滝|12=draft}}, and the renowned {{ill|Miya Water|ja|宮水|12=draft}} used in brewing by the Nada Gōgō.
== History == The shrine’s sacred rock formation is believed to be a man-made structure from the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代|12=draft}}, possibly constructed around 5,000 BCE. It was used by early Jōmon people for religious practices.
According to legend, the shrine was once the {{ill|Okunomiya|ja|奥宮|12=draft}} (inner shrine) of {{ill|Hirota Shrine|ja|廣田神社|12=draft}} and was also associated with the {{ill|Ara-matsuri-no-miya|ja|荒祭宮|12=draft}} of the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|12=draft}}.
The current sacred site is managed collaboratively by {{ill|Taimon-ji Temple|ja|多聞寺 (神戸市北区)|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Rokkō Hime Society|ja|六甲比命講|12=draft}}, with rituals such as autumn goma fire ceremonies conducted jointly.
== Recent Developments == In recent years, the shrine has gained popularity as a {{ill|power spot|ja|パワースポット|12=draft}}. Various groups now hold events near the sacred rocks, and visitors can obtain shrine-related materials, such as {{ill|goshuin|ja|御朱印|12=draft}}, talismans, and photos of sacred imagery.
== Access == === By Car === From {{ill|Takarazuka|ja|宝塚|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nishinomiya|ja|西宮|12=draft}}, or {{ill|Kobe|ja|神戸|12=draft}} via Hyōgo Prefectural Route 16, head toward {{ill|Rokkō Athletic Park GREENIA|ja|六甲山アスレチックパークGREENIA|12=draft}}. Parking is available near the shrine, with additional paid parking at GREENIA.
=== By Public Transportation === From {{ill|JR Shin-Kobe Station|ja|新神戸駅|12=draft}}, take the subway to {{ill|Sannomiya|ja|三宮|12=draft}}. Transfer to JR or Hankyū lines to reach {{ill|Rokkō Station|ja|六甲駅|12=draft}}. From there, take the bus to Rokkō Cable Car Station, ascend to Rokkōzan Station, and proceed to the shrine.
== Photo Gallery == <gallery> File:六甲比命大善神の御神体.JPG|Sacred rock formation of Rokkō Hime Daizen Shrine File:六甲山 心経岩.JPG|Shingyō Rock File:Rokkohime shrine, Kobe 002.jpg|Approach to the shrine (winter) </gallery>

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Rokko Hime Daizen Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= [[File:Rokkohime shrine, Kobe.jpg|280px|The inner shrine of Tamonji, Rokko Hime Shrine]] |location= {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kobe|ja|神戸市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nada Ward|ja|灘区|12=draft}}, Rokkōyama Town |Latitude degrees = 34 | Latitude minutes = 45 | Latitude seconds = 58.72 |Longitude degrees = 135 | Longitude minutes = 14 | Longitude seconds = 21.91 |Map= Kobe#Japan Hyogo |deity= Rokko Hime Daizen Kami ({{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|12=draft}}) |Sacred object= {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座|12=draft}} |Shrine rank= |Established= |Main hall= |Other names= |Pilgrimage sites= |festival= |Rituals= }} '''Rokko Hime Daizen Shrine''' (六甲比命大善神社, Rokkō Hime Daizen Jinja) is a Shinto shrine located in Rokkōyama Town, {{ill|Nada Ward|ja|灘区|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kobe|ja|神戸市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県|12=draft}}. It enshrines an awe-inspiring {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座|12=draft}} (sacred rock formation) as its sacred object.
Situated near the ridge of Mount Rokkō, the shrine is close to another sacred rock associated with {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|天穂日命|12=draft}}, located within the Rokkō Mountain Country House grounds.
The main sacred object, a grand Iwakura known as Yōga Iwa, is nestled between the areas of Un-ga Iwa and Shinkyō Iwa. The shrine serves as the inner sanctuary of {{ill|Tamonji|ja|多聞寺|12=draft}}, a temple founded in the 7th century by the Indian ascetic {{ill|Hōdō Sennin|ja|法道仙人|12=draft}} in the Karato district of northern Kobe.<ref>Makoto Tanabe, *Legends of Kobe*, Kobe Shimbun General Publishing Center.</ref>
== deity == * Rokko Hime Daizen Kami ({{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天|12=draft}}/{{ill|Kichijōten|ja|吉祥天|12=draft}}), also identified with Tsu Kisakaki Itsu-no-mitama Amasakaru Mukatsu-hime ({{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|12=draft}}).
The name of the enshrined deity has evolved alongside changes in the naming of Mount Rokkō. As a water deity, the kami's virtues are prominently evident in the region surrounding Mount Rokkō, including Japan's three famous hot springs (Arima Onsen), the renowned Nunobiki Falls (source of Kobe Water), and the legendary Miyamizu water of Nada Gō.
Historically, Mount Rokkō was referred to by the kanji 武庫 (Mukō), pronounced "Mukō-yama" or "Mukō-san," and in earlier times as "Mukatsu-mine," as noted in {{ill|Kamo no Mabuchi|ja|賀茂真淵|12=draft}}'s *Kanji-kō*. This suggests that the deity was originally known as Mukatsu-hime and later as Mukō-hime before evolving to its current name. The reading "Mukatsu" for "Rokkō" further su11orts this hypothesis.
Mount Rokkō was once part of the shrine territory of {{ill|Hirota Shrine|ja|廣田神社|12=draft}}.<ref>*Nishinomiya Shrine*, Gakuseisha, p. 53.</ref> The deity enshrined at Hirota Shrine, Tsu Kisakaki Itsu-no-mitama Amasakaru Mukatsu-hime (another name for {{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|12=draft}}), shares the name with the ancient Mukatsu-mine, aligning the two sites historically and theologically.
== History == The sacred Iwakura of Rokko Hime Shrine is believed to be a man-made structure constructed by stacking massive stones, dating back to the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代|12=draft}}. It is speculated to have been created around the Middle Jōmon period (circa 3000 BCE).
The shrine was historically associated with the Four Oni family of {{ill|En no Gyōja|ja|役行者|12=draft}}, who managed the western Rokkō mountains after migrating from the {{ill|Dōsen village|ja|洞川|12=draft}} in {{ill|Tenkawa Village|ja|天川村|12=draft}}.
According to the Rokko Hime Association, the shrine once served as an auxiliary shrine to Hirota Shrine and was also linked to the {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|12=draft}}, a sub-shrine of the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|lt=inner shrine|ja|皇大神宮|12=draft}} of {{ill|Ise Jingu|ja|伊勢神宮|12=draft}}. The association posits that the deity Rokko Hime is synonymous with Seoritsu-hime and attributes the name "Rokkō" to the presence of this sacred site. These interpretations are informed by the ancient texts of {{ill|Hotsuma Tsutae|ja|ホツマツタエ|12=draft}}.
The shrine is regarded as the primary sanctuary for worshiping Seoritsu-hime.
In the 7th century, the Indian monk {{ill|Hōdō Sennin|ja|法道仙人|12=draft}} founded {{ill|Tamonji|ja|多聞寺|12=draft}} on Mount Rokkō. The Buddhist deity {{ill|Bishamonten|ja|毘沙門天|12=draft}} (Vaiśravaṇa) descended upon the rock formation Un-ga Iwa within the shrine's precincts, establishing its connection to the temple.
During the Edo period, the shrine and the temple were jointly managed. However, with the Meiji government’s {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離|12=draft}} policy, the sacred sites became administratively separated, though they remain closely associated today.
Currently, the shrine is managed jointly by Tamonji and the Rokko Hime Association. Annual autumn rituals include Goma fire offerings conducted by the temple's head priest and the association.
== Recent Developments == In recent years, Rokko Hime Shrine has gained recognition as a {{ill|Power spot|ja|パワースポット|12=draft}}, attracting numerous visitors. Various group events are held in front of Shinkyō Iwa. The shrine's offerings include detailed pamphlets, Goshuin stamps, talismans dedicated to Rokko Hime Daizen Kami, charms for Seoritsu-hime, musical water bells, and decorative plaques featuring dragons or Kichijōten.
== Access == * By car: Accessible via the Rokko Mountain Roads from Takarazuka, Nishinomiya, or Ashiya. Visitors can aim for the nearby Rokko Mountain Athletic Park or Snow Park, with parking available near the shrine. * By public transport: From JR Shin-Kobe Station, take the subway to Sannomiya, then transfer to JR Tokaido Line for Rokko Station. From there, the Rokko Cable Car and mountain buses provide access to the shrine.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Rokkohime shrine, Kobe 002.jpg|Winter a11roach to the shrine File:六甲山 心経岩.JPG|Shinkyō Iwa File:六甲比命大善神拝殿 額.JPG|Plaque at the main shrine File:六甲比命大善神の御神体.JPG|Sacred Iwakura of Rokko Hime Daizen Shrine </gallery>
== References == <references/>

The name of the enshrined deity has evolved alongside changes in the naming of Mount Rokkō. As a water deity, the kami's virtues are prominently evident in the region surrounding Mount Rokkō, including Japan's three famous hot springs (Arima Onsen), the renowned Nunobiki Falls (source of Kobe Water), and the legendary Miyamizu water of Nada Gō.
Historically, Mount Rokkō was referred to by the kanji 武庫 (Mukō), pronounced "Mukō-yama" or "Mukō-san," and in earlier times as "Mukatsu-mine," as noted in {{ill|Kamo no Mabuchi|ja|賀茂真淵|12=draft}}'s *Kanji-kō*. This suggests that the deity was originally known as Mukatsu-hime and later as Mukō-hime before evolving to its current name. The reading "Mukatsu" for "Rokkō" further su11orts this hypothesis.
Mount Rokkō was once part of the shrine territory of {{ill|Hirota Shrine|ja|廣田神社|12=draft}}.<ref>*Nishinomiya Shrine*, Gakuseisha, p. 53.</ref> The deity enshrined at Hirota Shrine, Tsu Kisakaki Itsu-no-mitama Amasakaru Mukatsu-hime (another name for {{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|12=draft}}), shares the name with the ancient Mukatsu-mine, aligning the two sites historically and theologically.
== History == The sacred Iwakura of Rokko Hime Shrine is believed to be a man-made structure constructed by stacking massive stones, dating back to the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代|12=draft}}. It is speculated to have been created around the Middle Jōmon period (circa 3000 BCE).
The shrine was historically associated with the Four Oni family of {{ill|En no Gyōja|ja|役行者|12=draft}}, who managed the western Rokkō mountains after migrating from the {{ill|Dōsen village|ja|洞川|12=draft}} in {{ill|Tenkawa Village|ja|天川村|12=draft}}.
According to the Rokko Hime Association, the shrine once served as an auxiliary shrine to Hirota Shrine and was also linked to the {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|12=draft}}, a sub-shrine of the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|lt=inner shrine|ja|皇大神宮|12=draft}} of {{ill|Ise Jingu|ja|伊勢神宮|12=draft}}. The association posits that the deity Rokko Hime is synonymous with Seoritsu-hime and attributes the name "Rokkō" to the presence of this sacred site. These interpretations are informed by the ancient texts of {{ill|Hotsuma Tsutae|ja|ホツマツタエ|12=draft}}.
The shrine is regarded as the primary sanctuary for worshiping Seoritsu-hime.
In the 7th century, the Indian monk {{ill|Hōdō Sennin|ja|法道仙人|12=draft}} founded {{ill|Tamonji|ja|多聞寺|12=draft}} on Mount Rokkō. The Buddhist deity {{ill|Bishamonten|ja|毘沙門天|12=draft}} (Vaiśravaṇa) descended upon the rock formation Un-ga Iwa within the shrine's precincts, establishing its connection to the temple.
During the Edo period, the shrine and the temple were jointly managed. However, with the Meiji government’s {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離|12=draft}} policy, the sacred sites became administratively separated, though they remain closely associated today.
Currently, the shrine is managed jointly by Tamonji and the Rokko Hime Association. Annual autumn rituals include Goma fire offerings conducted by the temple's head priest and the association.
== Recent Developments == In recent years, Rokko Hime Shrine has gained recognition as a {{ill|Power spot|ja|パワースポット|12=draft}}, attracting numerous visitors. Various group events are held in front of Shinkyō Iwa. The shrine's offerings include detailed pamphlets, Goshuin stamps, talismans dedicated to Rokko Hime Daizen Kami, charms for Seoritsu-hime, musical water bells, and decorative plaques featuring dragons or Kichijōten.
== Access == * By car: Accessible via the Rokko Mountain Roads from Takarazuka, Nishinomiya, or Ashiya. Visitors can aim for the nearby Rokko Mountain Athletic Park or Snow Park, with parking available near the shrine. * By public transport: From JR Shin-Kobe Station, take the subway to Sannomiya, then transfer to JR Tokaido Line for Rokko Station. From there, the Rokko Cable Car and mountain buses provide access to the shrine.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Rokkohime shrine, Kobe 002.jpg|Winter a11roach to the shrine File:六甲山 心経岩.JPG|Shinkyō Iwa File:六甲比命大善神拝殿 額.JPG|Plaque at the main shrine File:六甲比命大善神の御神体.JPG|Sacred Iwakura of Rokko Hime Daizen Shrine </gallery>
== References == <references/>
== See also == * {{ill|Tamonji|ja|多聞寺 (神戸市北区)|12=draft}} * {{ill|Mount Rokko|ja|六甲山|12=draft}} * {{ill|Hirota Shrine|ja|廣田神社|12=draft}} * {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天|12=draft}} * {{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|12=draft}} * {{ill|En no Gyōja|ja|役行者|12=draft}} * {{ill|Rokkō Shugen|ja|六甲修験|12=draft}}
== External links == * [https://rokkouhime.wordpress.com/ Official website of Rokko Hime Shrine]

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Rokko Hime Daizen Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= [[File:Rokkohime shrine, Kobe.jpg|280px|The inner shrine of Tamonji, Rokko Hime Shrine]] |location= {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kobe|ja|神戸市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nada Ward|ja|灘区|12=draft}}, Rokkōyama Town |Latitude degrees = 34 | Latitude minutes = 45 | Latitude seconds = 58.72 |Longitude degrees = 135 | Longitude minutes = 14 | Longitude seconds = 21.91 |Map= Kobe#Japan Hyogo |deity= Rokko Hime Daizen Kami ({{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|12=draft}}) |Sacred object= {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座|12=draft}} |Shrine rank= |Established= |Main hall= |Other names= |Pilgrimage sites= |festival= |Rituals= }} '''Rokko Hime Daizen Shrine''' (六甲比命大善神社, Rokkō Hime Daizen Jinja) is a Shinto shrine located in Rokkōyama Town, {{ill|Nada Ward|ja|灘区|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kobe|ja|神戸市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県|12=draft}}. It enshrines an awe-inspiring {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座|12=draft}} (sacred rock formation) as its sacred object.
Situated near the ridge of Mount Rokkō, the shrine is close to another sacred rock associated with {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|天穂日命|12=draft}}, located within the Rokkō Mountain Country House grounds.
The main sacred object, a grand Iwakura known as Yōga Iwa, is nestled between the areas of Un-ga Iwa and Shinkyō Iwa. The shrine serves as the inner sanctuary of {{ill|Tamonji|ja|多聞寺|12=draft}}, a temple founded in the 7th century by the Indian ascetic {{ill|Hōdō Sennin|ja|法道仙人|12=draft}} in the Karato district of northern Kobe.<ref>Makoto Tanabe, *Legends of Kobe*, Kobe Shimbun General Publishing Center.</ref>

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Rokkō Hime Daizen Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= [[File:Rokkohime shrine, Kobe.jpg|280px|Rokkō Hime Daizen Shrine, Oku-no-in of Taimon-ji Temple]] |location= {{ill|Nada-ku, Kobe|ja|灘区|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Rokkōzan Town|ja|六甲山町|12=draft}} |Latitude = 34 | Latitude_min = 45 | Latitude_sec = 58.72 |Longitude = 135 | Longitude_min = 14 | Longitude_sec = 21.91 |Map= Kobe#Japan Hyogo |deity= Rokkō Hime Daizen God ({{ill|Saeōrihime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|12=draft}}) |Sacred_object= {{ill|Iwakura|ja|磐座|12=draft}} (sacred rock) |Rank= |consecration_year= |Main_hall= |Other_names= |Special_associations= |Festival= |Rituals= }}
'''Rokkō Hime Daizen Shrine''' (六甲比命大善神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Nada-ku, Kobe|ja|灘区|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県|12=draft}}, known for its sacred {{ill|iwakura rock|ja|磐座|12=draft}} (rock formation) as the object of worship.
Situated near the ridgeline of {{ill|Mount Rokkō|ja|六甲山|12=draft}}, the shrine is close to other sacred sites such as the rock formation dedicated to {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|天穂日命|12=draft}} within the Rokkō Country House grounds.
== deity == * Main deity - Rokkō Hime Daizen God (identified as {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kichijōten|ja|吉祥天|12=draft}}), also known as Tsukisakaki Itsu-no-Mitama Amasakaru Mukatsu Hime (撞賢木厳魂天疎向津姫, {{ill|Saeōrihime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|12=draft}}).
The deity’s name has evolved along with the mountain’s name, reflecting its role as a water god influencing the region’s renowned springs such as {{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nunobiki Waterfall|ja|布引の滝|12=draft}}, and the renowned {{ill|Miya Water|ja|宮水|12=draft}} used in brewing by the Nada Gōgō.
== History == The shrine’s sacred rock formation is believed to be a man-made structure from the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代|12=draft}}, possibly constructed around 5,000 BCE. It was used by early Jōmon people for religious practices.
According to legend, the shrine was once the {{ill|Okunomiya|ja|奥宮|12=draft}} (inner shrine) of {{ill|Hirota Shrine|ja|廣田神社|12=draft}} and was also associated with the {{ill|Ara-matsuri-no-miya|ja|荒祭宮|12=draft}} of the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|12=draft}}.
The current sacred site is managed collaboratively by {{ill|Taimon-ji Temple|ja|多聞寺 (神戸市北区)|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Rokkō Hime Society|ja|六甲比命講|12=draft}}, with rituals such as autumn goma fire ceremonies conducted jointly.
== Recent Developments == In recent years, the shrine has gained popularity as a {{ill|power spot|ja|パワースポット|12=draft}}. Various groups now hold events near the sacred rocks, and visitors can obtain shrine-related materials, such as {{ill|goshuin|ja|御朱印|12=draft}}, talismans, and photos of sacred imagery.
== Access == === By Car === From {{ill|Takarazuka|ja|宝塚|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nishinomiya|ja|西宮|12=draft}}, or {{ill|Kobe|ja|神戸|12=draft}} via Hyōgo Prefectural Route 16, head toward {{ill|Rokkō Athletic Park GREENIA|ja|六甲山アスレチックパークGREENIA|12=draft}}. Parking is available near the shrine, with additional paid parking at GREENIA.
=== By Public Transportation === From {{ill|JR Shin-Kobe Station|ja|新神戸駅|12=draft}}, take the subway to {{ill|Sannomiya|ja|三宮|12=draft}}. Transfer to JR or Hankyū lines to reach {{ill|Rokkō Station|ja|六甲駅|12=draft}}. From there, take the bus to Rokkō Cable Car Station, ascend to Rokkōzan Station, and proceed to the shrine.
== Photo Gallery == <gallery> File:六甲比命大善神の御神体.JPG|Sacred rock formation of Rokkō Hime Daizen Shrine File:六甲山 心経岩.JPG|Shingyō Rock File:Rokkohime shrine, Kobe 002.jpg|Approach to the shrine (winter) </gallery>

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Rokkō Hime Daizen Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= [[File:Rokkohime shrine, Kobe.jpg|280px|Rokkō Hime Daizen Shrine, Oku-no-in of Taimon-ji Temple]] |location= {{ill|Nada-ku, Kobe|ja|灘区|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Rokkōzan Town|ja|六甲山町|12=draft}} |Latitude = 34 | Latitude_min = 45 | Latitude_sec = 58.72 |Longitude = 135 | Longitude_min = 14 | Longitude_sec = 21.91 |Map= Kobe#Japan Hyogo |deity= Rokkō Hime Daizen God ({{ill|Saeōrihime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|12=draft}}) |Sacred_object= {{ill|Iwakura|ja|磐座|12=draft}} (sacred rock) |Rank= |consecration_year= |Main_hall= |Other_names= |Special_associations= |Festival= |Rituals= }}
'''Rokkō Hime Daizen Shrine''' (六甲比命大善神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Nada-ku, Kobe|ja|灘区|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県|12=draft}}, known for its sacred {{ill|iwakura rock|ja|磐座|12=draft}} (rock formation) as the object of worship.
Situated near the ridgeline of {{ill|Mount Rokkō|ja|六甲山|12=draft}}, the shrine is close to other sacred sites such as the rock formation dedicated to {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|天穂日命|12=draft}} within the Rokkō Country House grounds.
== deity == * Main deity - Rokkō Hime Daizen God (identified as {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kichijōten|ja|吉祥天|12=draft}}), also known as Tsukisakaki Itsu-no-Mitama Amasakaru Mukatsu Hime (撞賢木厳魂天疎向津姫, {{ill|Saeōrihime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|12=draft}}).
The deity’s name has evolved along with the mountain’s name, reflecting its role as a water god influencing the region’s renowned springs such as {{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nunobiki Waterfall|ja|布引の滝|12=draft}}, and the renowned {{ill|Miya Water|ja|宮水|12=draft}} used in brewing by the Nada Gōgō.
== History == The shrine’s sacred rock formation is believed to be a man-made structure from the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代|12=draft}}, possibly constructed around 5,000 BCE. It was used by early Jōmon people for religious practices.
According to legend, the shrine was once the {{ill|Okunomiya|ja|奥宮|12=draft}} (inner shrine) of {{ill|Hirota Shrine|ja|廣田神社|12=draft}} and was also associated with the {{ill|Ara-matsuri-no-miya|ja|荒祭宮|12=draft}} of the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|12=draft}}.
The current sacred site is managed collaboratively by {{ill|Taimon-ji Temple|ja|多聞寺 (神戸市北区)|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Rokkō Hime Society|ja|六甲比命講|12=draft}}, with rituals such as autumn goma fire ceremonies conducted jointly.
== Recent Developments == In recent years, the shrine has gained popularity as a {{ill|power spot|ja|パワースポット|12=draft}}. Various groups now hold events near the sacred rocks, and visitors can obtain shrine-related materials, such as {{ill|goshuin|ja|御朱印|12=draft}}, talismans, and photos of sacred imagery.
== Access == === By Car === From {{ill|Takarazuka|ja|宝塚|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nishinomiya|ja|西宮|12=draft}}, or {{ill|Kobe|ja|神戸|12=draft}} via Hyōgo Prefectural Route 16, head toward {{ill|Rokkō Athletic Park GREENIA|ja|六甲山アスレチックパークGREENIA|12=draft}}. Parking is available near the shrine, with additional paid parking at GREENIA.
=== By Public Transportation === From {{ill|JR Shin-Kobe Station|ja|新神戸駅|12=draft}}, take the subway to {{ill|Sannomiya|ja|三宮|12=draft}}. Transfer to JR or Hankyū lines to reach {{ill|Rokkō Station|ja|六甲駅|12=draft}}. From there, take the bus to Rokkō Cable Car Station, ascend to Rokkōzan Station, and proceed to the shrine.
== Photo Gallery == <gallery> File:六甲比命大善神の御神体.JPG|Sacred rock formation of Rokkō Hime Daizen Shrine File:六甲山 心経岩.JPG|Shingyō Rock File:Rokkohime shrine, Kobe 002.jpg|Approach to the shrine (winter) </gallery>

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Rokko Hime Daizen Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= [[File:Rokkohime shrine, Kobe.jpg|280px|The inner shrine of Tamonji, Rokko Hime Shrine]] |location= {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kobe|ja|神戸市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nada Ward|ja|灘区|12=draft}}, Rokkōyama Town |Latitude degrees = 34 | Latitude minutes = 45 | Latitude seconds = 58.72 |Longitude degrees = 135 | Longitude minutes = 14 | Longitude seconds = 21.91 |Map= Kobe#Japan Hyogo |deity= Rokko Hime Daizen Kami ({{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|12=draft}}) |Sacred object= {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座|12=draft}} |Shrine rank= |Established= |Main hall= |Other names= |Pilgrimage sites= |festival= |Rituals= }} '''Rokko Hime Daizen Shrine''' (六甲比命大善神社, Rokkō Hime Daizen Jinja) is a Shinto shrine located in Rokkōyama Town, {{ill|Nada Ward|ja|灘区|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kobe|ja|神戸市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県|12=draft}}. It enshrines an awe-inspiring {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座|12=draft}} (sacred rock formation) as its sacred object.
Situated near the ridge of Mount Rokkō, the shrine is close to another sacred rock associated with {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|天穂日命|12=draft}}, located within the Rokkō Mountain Country House grounds.
The main sacred object, a grand Iwakura known as Yōga Iwa, is nestled between the areas of Un-ga Iwa and Shinkyō Iwa. The shrine serves as the inner sanctuary of {{ill|Tamonji|ja|多聞寺|12=draft}}, a temple founded in the 7th century by the Indian ascetic {{ill|Hōdō Sennin|ja|法道仙人|12=draft}} in the Karato district of northern Kobe.<ref>Makoto Tanabe, *Legends of Kobe*, Kobe Shimbun General Publishing Center.</ref>
== deity == * Rokko Hime Daizen Kami ({{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天|12=draft}}/{{ill|Kichijōten|ja|吉祥天|12=draft}}), also identified with Tsu Kisakaki Itsu-no-mitama Amasakaru Mukatsu-hime ({{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|12=draft}}).
The name of the enshrined deity has evolved alongside changes in the naming of Mount Rokkō. As a water deity, the kami's virtues are prominently evident in the region surrounding Mount Rokkō, including Japan's three famous hot springs (Arima Onsen), the renowned Nunobiki Falls (source of Kobe Water), and the legendary Miyamizu water of Nada Gō.
Historically, Mount Rokkō was referred to by the kanji 武庫 (Mukō), pronounced "Mukō-yama" or "Mukō-san," and in earlier times as "Mukatsu-mine," as noted in {{ill|Kamo no Mabuchi|ja|賀茂真淵|12=draft}}'s *Kanji-kō*. This suggests that the deity was originally known as Mukatsu-hime and later as Mukō-hime before evolving to its current name. The reading "Mukatsu" for "Rokkō" further supports this hypothesis.
Mount Rokkō was once part of the shrine territory of {{ill|Hirota Shrine|ja|廣田神社|12=draft}}.<ref>*Nishinomiya Shrine*, Gakuseisha, p. 53.</ref> The deity enshrined at Hirota Shrine, Tsu Kisakaki Itsu-no-mitama Amasakaru Mukatsu-hime (another name for {{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|12=draft}}), shares the name with the ancient Mukatsu-mine, aligning the two sites historically and theologically.
== History == The sacred Iwakura of Rokko Hime Shrine is believed to be a man-made structure constructed by stacking massive stones, dating back to the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代|12=draft}}. It is speculated to have been created around the Middle Jōmon period (circa 3000 BCE).
The shrine was historically associated with the Four Oni family of {{ill|En no Gyōja|ja|役行者|12=draft}}, who managed the western Rokkō mountains after migrating from the {{ill|Dōsen village|ja|洞川|12=draft}} in {{ill|Tenkawa Village|ja|天川村|12=draft}}.
According to the Rokko Hime Association, the shrine once served as an auxiliary shrine to Hirota Shrine and was also linked to the {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|12=draft}}, a sub-shrine of the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|lt=inner shrine|ja|皇大神宮|12=draft}} of {{ill|Ise Jingu|ja|伊勢神宮|12=draft}}. The association posits that the deity Rokko Hime is synonymous with Seoritsu-hime and attributes the name "Rokkō" to the presence of this sacred site. These interpretations are informed by the ancient texts of {{ill|Hotsuma Tsutae|ja|ホツマツタエ|12=draft}}.
The shrine is regarded as the primary sanctuary for worshiping Seoritsu-hime.
In the 7th century, the Indian monk {{ill|Hōdō Sennin|ja|法道仙人|12=draft}} founded {{ill|Tamonji|ja|多聞寺|12=draft}} on Mount Rokkō. The Buddhist deity {{ill|Bishamonten|ja|毘沙門天|12=draft}} (Vaiśravaṇa) descended upon the rock formation Un-ga Iwa within the shrine's precincts, establishing its connection to the temple.
During the Edo period, the shrine and the temple were jointly managed. However, with the Meiji government’s {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離|12=draft}} policy, the sacred sites became administratively separated, though they remain closely associated today.
Currently, the shrine is managed jointly by Tamonji and the Rokko Hime Association. Annual autumn rituals include Goma fire offerings conducted by the temple's head priest and the association.
== Recent Developments == In recent years, Rokko Hime Shrine has gained recognition as a {{ill|Power spot|ja|パワースポット|12=draft}}, attracting numerous visitors. Various group events are held in front of Shinkyō Iwa. The shrine's offerings include detailed pamphlets, Goshuin stamps, talismans dedicated to Rokko Hime Daizen Kami, charms for Seoritsu-hime, musical water bells, and decorative plaques featuring dragons or Kichijōten.
== Access == * By car: Accessible via the Rokko Mountain Roads from Takarazuka, Nishinomiya, or Ashiya. Visitors can aim for the nearby Rokko Mountain Athletic Park or Snow Park, with parking available near the shrine. * By public transport: From JR Shin-Kobe Station, take the subway to Sannomiya, then transfer to JR Tokaido Line for Rokko Station. From there, the Rokko Cable Car and mountain buses provide access to the shrine.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Rokkohime shrine, Kobe 002.jpg|Winter approach to the shrine File:六甲山 心経岩.JPG|Shinkyō Iwa File:六甲比命大善神拝殿 額.JPG|Plaque at the main shrine File:六甲比命大善神の御神体.JPG|Sacred Iwakura of Rokko Hime Daizen Shrine </gallery>
== References == <references/>
== See also == * {{ill|Tamonji|ja|多聞寺 (神戸市北区)|12=draft}} * {{ill|Mount Rokko|ja|六甲山|12=draft}} * {{ill|Hirota Shrine|ja|廣田神社|12=draft}} * {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天|12=draft}} * {{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|12=draft}} * {{ill|En no Gyōja|ja|役行者|12=draft}} * {{ill|Rokkō Shugen|ja|六甲修験|12=draft}}

Historically, Mount Rokkō was referred to by the kanji 武庫 (Mukō), pronounced "Mukō-yama" or "Mukō-san," and in earlier times as "Mukatsu-mine," as noted in {{ill|Kamo no Mabuchi|ja|賀茂真淵|12=draft}}'s *Kanji-kō*. This suggests that the deity was originally known as Mukatsu-hime and later as Mukō-hime before evolving to its current name. The reading "Mukatsu" for "Rokkō" further supports this hypothesis.
Mount Rokkō was once part of the shrine territory of {{ill|Hirota Shrine|ja|廣田神社|12=draft}}.<ref>*Nishinomiya Shrine*, Gakuseisha, p. 53.</ref> The deity enshrined at Hirota Shrine, Tsu Kisakaki Itsu-no-mitama Amasakaru Mukatsu-hime (another name for {{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|12=draft}}), shares the name with the ancient Mukatsu-mine, aligning the two sites historically and theologically.
== History == The sacred Iwakura of Rokko Hime Shrine is believed to be a man-made structure constructed by stacking massive stones, dating back to the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代|12=draft}}. It is speculated to have been created around the Middle Jōmon period (circa 3000 BCE).
The shrine was historically associated with the Four Oni family of {{ill|En no Gyōja|ja|役行者|12=draft}}, who managed the western Rokkō mountains after migrating from the {{ill|Dōsen village|ja|洞川|12=draft}} in {{ill|Tenkawa Village|ja|天川村|12=draft}}.
According to the Rokko Hime Association, the shrine once served as an auxiliary shrine to Hirota Shrine and was also linked to the {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|12=draft}}, a sub-shrine of the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|lt=inner shrine|ja|皇大神宮|12=draft}} of {{ill|Ise Jingu|ja|伊勢神宮|12=draft}}. The association posits that the deity Rokko Hime is synonymous with Seoritsu-hime and attributes the name "Rokkō" to the presence of this sacred site. These interpretations are informed by the ancient texts of {{ill|Hotsuma Tsutae|ja|ホツマツタエ|12=draft}}.
The shrine is regarded as the primary sanctuary for worshiping Seoritsu-hime.
In the 7th century, the Indian monk {{ill|Hōdō Sennin|ja|法道仙人|12=draft}} founded {{ill|Tamonji|ja|多聞寺|12=draft}} on Mount Rokkō. The Buddhist deity {{ill|Bishamonten|ja|毘沙門天|12=draft}} (Vaiśravaṇa) descended upon the rock formation Un-ga Iwa within the shrine's precincts, establishing its connection to the temple.
During the Edo period, the shrine and the temple were jointly managed. However, with the Meiji government’s {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離|12=draft}} policy, the sacred sites became administratively separated, though they remain closely associated today.
Currently, the shrine is managed jointly by Tamonji and the Rokko Hime Association. Annual autumn rituals include Goma fire offerings conducted by the temple's head priest and the association.
== Recent Developments == In recent years, Rokko Hime Shrine has gained recognition as a {{ill|Power spot|ja|パワースポット|12=draft}}, attracting numerous visitors. Various group events are held in front of Shinkyō Iwa. The shrine's offerings include detailed pamphlets, Goshuin stamps, talismans dedicated to Rokko Hime Daizen Kami, charms for Seoritsu-hime, musical water bells, and decorative plaques featuring dragons or Kichijōten.
== Access == * By car: Accessible via the Rokko Mountain Roads from Takarazuka, Nishinomiya, or Ashiya. Visitors can aim for the nearby Rokko Mountain Athletic Park or Snow Park, with parking available near the shrine. * By public transport: From JR Shin-Kobe Station, take the subway to Sannomiya, then transfer to JR Tokaido Line for Rokko Station. From there, the Rokko Cable Car and mountain buses provide access to the shrine.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Rokkohime shrine, Kobe 002.jpg|Winter approach to the shrine File:六甲山 心経岩.JPG|Shinkyō Iwa File:六甲比命大善神拝殿 額.JPG|Plaque at the main shrine File:六甲比命大善神の御神体.JPG|Sacred Iwakura of Rokko Hime Daizen Shrine </gallery>

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Rokkō Hime Daizen Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= [[File:Rokkohime shrine, Kobe.jpg|280px|Rokkō Hime Daizen Shrine, Oku-no-in of Taimon-ji Temple]] |location= {{ill|Nada-ku, Kobe|ja|灘区|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Rokkōzan Town|ja|六甲山町|12=draft}} |Latitude = 34 | Latitude_min = 45 | Latitude_sec = 58.72 |Longitude = 135 | Longitude_min = 14 | Longitude_sec = 21.91 |Map= Kobe#Japan Hyogo |deity= Rokkō Hime Daizen God ({{ill|Saeōrihime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|12=draft}}) |Sacred_object= {{ill|Iwakura|ja|磐座|12=draft}} (sacred rock) |Rank= |consecration_year= |Main_hall= |Other_names= |Special_associations= |Festival= |Rituals= }}
'''Rokkō Hime Daizen Shrine''' (六甲比命大善神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Nada-ku, Kobe|ja|灘区|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県|12=draft}}, known for its sacred {{ill|iwakura rock|ja|磐座|12=draft}} (rock formation) as the object of worship.
Situated near the ridgeline of {{ill|Mount Rokkō|ja|六甲山|12=draft}}, the shrine is close to other sacred sites such as the rock formation dedicated to {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|天穂日命|12=draft}} within the Rokkō Country House grounds.
== deity == * Main deity - Rokkō Hime Daizen God (identified as {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kichijōten|ja|吉祥天|12=draft}}), also known as Tsukisakaki Itsu-no-Mitama Amasakaru Mukatsu Hime (撞賢木厳魂天疎向津姫, {{ill|Saeōrihime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|12=draft}}).
The deity’s name has evolved along with the mountain’s name, reflecting its role as a water god influencing the region’s renowned springs such as {{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nunobiki Waterfall|ja|布引の滝|12=draft}}, and the renowned {{ill|Miya Water|ja|宮水|12=draft}} used in brewing by the Nada Gōgō.
== History == The shrine’s sacred rock formation is believed to be a man-made structure from the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代|12=draft}}, possibly constructed around 5,000 BCE. It was used by early Jōmon people for religious practices.
According to legend, the shrine was once the {{ill|Okunomiya|ja|奥宮|12=draft}} (inner shrine) of {{ill|Hirota Shrine|ja|廣田神社|12=draft}} and was also associated with the {{ill|Ara-matsuri-no-miya|ja|荒祭宮|12=draft}} of the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|12=draft}}.
The current sacred site is managed collaboratively by {{ill|Taimon-ji Temple|ja|多聞寺 (神戸市北区)|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Rokkō Hime Society|ja|六甲比命講|12=draft}}, with rituals such as autumn goma fire ceremonies conducted jointly.
== Recent Developments == In recent years, the shrine has gained popularity as a {{ill|power spot|ja|パワースポット|12=draft}}. Various groups now hold events near the sacred rocks, and visitors can obtain shrine-related materials, such as {{ill|goshuin|ja|御朱印|12=draft}}, talismans, and photos of sacred imagery.
== Access == === By Car === From {{ill|Takarazuka|ja|宝塚|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nishinomiya|ja|西宮|12=draft}}, or {{ill|Kobe|ja|神戸|12=draft}} via Hyōgo Prefectural Route 16, head toward {{ill|Rokkō Athletic Park GREENIA|ja|六甲山アスレチックパークGREENIA|12=draft}}. Parking is available near the shrine, with additional paid parking at GREENIA.
=== By Public Transportation === From {{ill|JR Shin-Kobe Station|ja|新神戸駅|12=draft}}, take the subway to {{ill|Sannomiya|ja|三宮|12=draft}}. Transfer to JR or Hankyū lines to reach {{ill|Rokkō Station|ja|六甲駅|12=draft}}. From there, take the bus to Rokkō Cable Car Station, ascend to Rokkōzan Station, and proceed to the shrine.
== Photo Gallery == <gallery> File:六甲比命大善神の御神体.JPG|Sacred rock formation of Rokkō Hime Daizen Shrine File:六甲山 心経岩.JPG|Shingyō Rock File:Rokkohime shrine, Kobe 002.jpg|Approach to the shrine (winter) </gallery>
== References == <references/> == See Also == * {{ill|Taimon-ji Temple|ja|多聞寺 (神戸市北区)|12=draft}} * {{ill|Mount Rokkō|ja|六甲山|12=draft}} * {{ill|Hirota Shrine|ja|廣田神社|12=draft}} * {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天|12=draft}} * {{ill|Saeōrihime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|12=draft}} * {{ill|En no Gyōja|ja|役行者|12=draft}} * {{ill|Rokkō Shugen|ja|六甲修験|12=draft}}
== External Links == * [https://rokkouhime.wordpress.com/ Official site of Rokkō Hime Shrine] * [{{NDLDC|864336}} Reference to historical documents]

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Rokko Hime Daizen Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= [[File:Rokkohime shrine, Kobe.jpg|280px|The inner shrine of Tamonji, Rokko Hime Shrine]] |location= {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kobe|ja|神戸市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nada Ward|ja|灘区|12=draft}}, Rokkōyama Town |Latitude degrees = 34 | Latitude minutes = 45 | Latitude seconds = 58.72 |Longitude degrees = 135 | Longitude minutes = 14 | Longitude seconds = 21.91 |Map= Kobe#Japan Hyogo |deity= Rokko Hime Daizen Kami ({{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|12=draft}}) |Sacred object= {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座|12=draft}} |Shrine rank= |Established= |Main hall= |Other names= |Pilgrimage sites= |festival= |Rituals= }} '''Rokko Hime Daizen Shrine''' (六甲比命大善神社, Rokkō Hime Daizen Jinja) is a Shinto shrine located in Rokkōyama Town, {{ill|Nada Ward|ja|灘区|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kobe|ja|神戸市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県|12=draft}}. It enshrines an awe-inspiring {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座|12=draft}} (sacred rock formation) as its sacred object.
Situated near the ridge of Mount Rokkō, the shrine is close to another sacred rock associated with {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|天穂日命|12=draft}}, located within the Rokkō Mountain Country House grounds.
The main sacred object, a grand Iwakura known as Yōga Iwa, is nestled between the areas of Un-ga Iwa and Shinkyō Iwa. The shrine serves as the inner sanctuary of {{ill|Tamonji|ja|多聞寺|12=draft}}, a temple founded in the 7th century by the Indian ascetic {{ill|Hōdō Sennin|ja|法道仙人|12=draft}} in the Karato district of northern Kobe.<ref>Makoto Tanabe, *Legends of Kobe*, Kobe Shimbun General Publishing Center.</ref>
== deity == * Rokko Hime Daizen Kami ({{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天|12=draft}}/{{ill|Kichijōten|ja|吉祥天|12=draft}}), also identified with Tsu Kisakaki Itsu-no-mitama Amasakaru Mukatsu-hime ({{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|12=draft}}).
The name of the enshrined deity has evolved alongside changes in the naming of Mount Rokkō. As a water deity, the kami's virtues are prominently evident in the region surrounding Mount Rokkō, including Japan's three famous hot springs (Arima Onsen), the renowned Nunobiki Falls (source of Kobe Water), and the legendary Miyamizu water of Nada Gō.

According to the Rokko Hime Association, the shrine once served as an auxiliary shrine to Hirota Shrine and was also linked to the {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|12=draft}}, a sub-shrine of the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|lt=inner shrine|ja|皇大神宮|12=draft}} of {{ill|Ise Jingu|ja|伊勢神宮|12=draft}}. The association posits that the deity Rokko Hime is synonymous with Seoritsu-hime and attributes the name "Rokkō" to the presence of this sacred site. These interpretations are informed by the ancient texts of {{ill|Hotsuma Tsutae|ja|ホツマツタエ|12=draft}}.
The shrine is regarded as the primary sanctuary for worshiping Seoritsu-hime.
In the 7th century, the Indian monk {{ill|Hōdō Sennin|ja|法道仙人|12=draft}} founded {{ill|Tamonji|ja|多聞寺|12=draft}} on Mount Rokkō. The Buddhist deity {{ill|Bishamonten|ja|毘沙門天|12=draft}} (Vaiśravaṇa) descended upon the rock formation Un-ga Iwa within the shrine's precincts, establishing its connection to the temple.
During the Edo period, the shrine and the temple were jointly managed. However, with the Meiji government’s {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離|12=draft}} policy, the sacred sites became administratively separated, though they remain closely associated today.

== Recent Developments == In recent years, Rokko Hime Shrine has gained recognition as a {{ill|Power spot|ja|パワースポット|12=draft}}, attracting numerous visitors. Visitors can aim for the nearby Rokko Mountain Athletic Park or Snow Park, with parking available near the shrine. * By public transport: From JR Shin-Kobe Station, take the subway to Sannomiya, then transfer to JR Tokaido Line for Rokko Station. From there, the Rokko Cable Car and mountain buses provide access to the shrine.

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Rokko Hime Daizen Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= [[File:Rokkohime shrine, Kobe.jpg|280px|The inner shrine of Tamonji, Rokko Hime Shrine]] |location= {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kobe|ja|神戸市|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nada Ward|ja|灘区|12=draft|12=draft}}, Rokkōyama Town |Latitude degrees = 34 | Latitude minutes = 45 | Latitude seconds = 58.72 |Longitude degrees = 135 | Longitude minutes = 14 | Longitude seconds = 21.91 |Map= Kobe#Japan Hyogo |deity= Rokko Hime Daizen Kami ({{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|12=draft|12=draft}}) |Sacred object= {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座|12=draft|12=draft}} |Shrine Rank= |Established= |Main hall= |Other names= |Pilgrimage sites= |festival= |Rituals= }} '''Rokko Hime Daizen Shrine''' (六甲比命大善神社, Rokkō Hime Daizen Jinja) is a Shinto shrine located in Rokkōyama Town, {{ill|Nada Ward|ja|灘区|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kobe|ja|神戸市|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県|12=draft|12=draft}}. It enshrines an awe-inspiring {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座|12=draft|12=draft}} (sacred rock formation) as its sacred object.
Situated near the ridge of Mount Rokkō, the shrine is close to another sacred rock associated with {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|天穂日命|12=draft|12=draft}}, located within the Rokkō Mountain Country House grounds.
The main sacred object, a grand Iwakura known as Yōga Iwa, is nestled between the areas of Un-ga Iwa and Shinkyō Iwa. The shrine serves as the inner sanctuary of {{ill|Tamonji|ja|多聞寺|12=draft|12=draft}}, a temple founded in the 7th century by the Indian ascetic {{ill|Hōdō Sennin|ja|法道仙人|12=draft|12=draft}} in the Karato district of northern Kobe.<ref>Makoto Tanabe, *Legends of Kobe*, Kobe Shimbun General Publishing Center.</ref>
== deity == * Rokko Hime Daizen Kami ({{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天|12=draft|12=draft}}/{{ill|Kichijōten|ja|吉祥天|12=draft|12=draft}}), also identified with Tsu Kisakaki Itsu-no-mitama Amasakaru Mukatsu-hime ({{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|12=draft|12=draft}}).
The name of the enshrined deity has evolved alongside changes in the naming of Mount Rokkō. As a water deity, the kami's virtues are prominently evident in the region surrounding Mount Rokkō, including Japan's three famous hot springs (Arima Onsen), the renowned Nunobiki Falls (source of Kobe Water), and the legendary Miyamizu water of Nada Gō.
Historically, Mount Rokkō was referred to by the kanji 武庫 (Mukō), pronounced "Mukō-yama" or "Mukō-san," and in earlier times as "Mukatsu-mine," as noted in {{ill|Kamo no Mabuchi|ja|賀茂真淵|12=draft|12=draft}}'s *Kanji-kō*. This suggests that the deity was originally known as Mukatsu-hime and later as Mukō-hime before evolving to its current name. The reading "Mukatsu" for "Rokkō" further supports this hypothesis.
Mount Rokkō was once part of the shrine territory of {{ill|Hirota Shrine|ja|廣田神社|12=draft|12=draft}}.<ref>*Nishinomiya Shrine*, Gakuseisha, p. 53.</ref> The deity enshrined at Hirota Shrine, Tsu Kisakaki Itsu-no-mitama Amasakaru Mukatsu-hime (another name for {{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|12=draft|12=draft}}), shares the name with the ancient Mukatsu-mine, aligning the two sites historically and theologically.
== History == The sacred Iwakura of Rokko Hime Shrine is believed to be a man-made structure constructed by stacking massive stones, dating back to the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代|12=draft|12=draft}}. It is speculated to have been created around the Middle Jōmon period (circa 3000 BCE).
The shrine was historically associated with the Four Oni family of {{ill|En no Gyōja|ja|役行者|12=draft|12=draft}}, who managed the western Rokkō mountains after migrating from the {{ill|Dōsen village|ja|洞川|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Tenkawa Village|ja|天川村|12=draft|12=draft}}.
According to the Rokko Hime Association, the shrine once served as an auxiliary shrine to Hirota Shrine and was also linked to the {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, a sub-shrine of the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|lt=inner shrine|ja|皇大神宮|12=draft|12=draft}} of {{ill|Ise Jingu|ja|伊勢神宮|12=draft|12=draft}}. The association posits that the deity Rokko Hime is synonymous with Seoritsu-hime and attributes the name "Rokkō" to the presence of this sacred site. These interpretations are informed by the ancient texts of {{ill|Hotsuma Tsutae|ja|ホツマツタエ|12=draft|12=draft}}.
The shrine is regarded as the primary sanctuary for worshiping Seoritsu-hime.
In the 7th century, the Indian monk {{ill|Hōdō Sennin|ja|法道仙人|12=draft|12=draft}} founded {{ill|Tamonji|ja|多聞寺|12=draft|12=draft}} on Mount Rokkō. The Buddhist deity {{ill|Bishamonten|ja|毘沙門天|12=draft|12=draft}} (Vaiśravaṇa) descended upon the rock formation Un-ga Iwa within the shrine's precincts, establishing its connection to the temple.
During the Edo period, the shrine and the temple were jointly managed. However, with the Meiji government’s {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離|12=draft|12=draft}} policy, the sacred sites became administratively separated, though they remain closely associated today.
Currently, the shrine is managed jointly by Tamonji and the Rokko Hime Association. Annual autumn rituals include Goma fire offerings conducted by the temple's head priest and the association.
== Recent Developments == In recent years, Rokko Hime Shrine has gained recognition as a {{ill|Power spot|ja|パワースポット|12=draft|12=draft}}, attracting numerous visitors. Various group events are held in front of Shinkyō Iwa. The shrine's offerings include detailed pamphlets, Goshuin stamps, talismans dedicated to Rokko Hime Daizen Kami, charms for Seoritsu-hime, musical water bells, and decorative plaques featuring dragons or Kichijōten.
== Access == * By car: Accessible via the Rokko Mountain Roads from Takarazuka, Nishinomiya, or Ashiya. Visitors can aim for the nearby Rokko Mountain Athletic Park or Snow Park, with parking available near the shrine. * By public transport: From JR Shin-Kobe Station, take the subway to Sannomiya, then transfer to JR Tokaido Line for Rokko Station. From there, the Rokko Cable Car and mountain buses provide access to the shrine.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Rokkohime shrine, Kobe 002.jpg|Winter approach to the shrine File:六甲山 心経岩.JPG|Shinkyō Iwa File:六甲比命大善神拝殿 額.JPG|Plaque at the main shrine File:六甲比命大善神の御神体.JPG|Sacred Iwakura of Rokko Hime Daizen Shrine </gallery>
== References == <references/>
== See also == * {{ill|Tamonji|ja|多聞寺 (神戸市北区)|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Mount Rokko|ja|六甲山|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Hirota Shrine|ja|廣田神社|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|En no Gyōja|ja|役行者|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Rokkō Shugen|ja|六甲修験|12=draft|12=draft}}
== External links == * [https://rokkouhime.wordpress.com/ Official website of Rokko Hime Shrine]

Visitors can aim for the nearby Rokko Mountain Athletic Park or Snow Park, with parking available near the shrine. * By public transport: From JR Shin-Kobe Station, take the subway to Sannomiya, then transfer to JR Tokaido Line for Rokko Station. From there, the Rokko Cable Car and mountain buses provide access to the shrine.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Rokkohime shrine, Kobe 002.jpg|Winter approach to the shrine File:六甲山 心経岩.JPG|Shinkyō Iwa File:六甲比命大善神拝殿 額.JPG|Plaque at the main shrine File:六甲比命大善神の御神体.JPG|Sacred Iwakura of Rokko Hime Daizen Shrine </gallery>

revision.comment: 116 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:六甲比命神社 up to rev 101753552
revision.text:
{{神社 |名称= 六甲比命大善神社 |画像= [[File:Rokkohime shrine, Kobe.jpg|280px|多聞寺奥の院、六甲比命神社]] |所在地 = [[兵庫県]][[神戸市]][[灘区]]六甲山町 |緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 45 | 緯度秒 = 58.72 |経度度 = 135 |経度分 = 14 | 経度秒 = 21.91 |地図= Kobe#Japan Hyogo |祭神= 六甲比命大善神([[瀬織津姫]]) |神体= [[磐座]] |社格= |創建= |本殿= |別名= |札所等= |例祭= |神事= }} '''六甲比命大善神社'''（ろっこうひめだいぜんじんじゃ）は、[[兵庫県]][[神戸市]][[灘区]][[六甲山町]]にある、荘厳な[[磐座]]をご神体とする神社である。
六甲山頂尾根付近に位置し、近くには六甲山カンツリーハウス敷地内に[[天穂日命]]の磐座もある。
仰臥岩、雲が岩、心経岩の中間地点に位置する壮大な磐座が御神体で、7世紀にインドから渡来した[[法道]]仙人が創建した吉祥院[[多聞寺 (神戸市北区)|多聞寺]]（神戸市北区唐櫃）の奥の院となっている<ref>『神戸の伝説』[[田辺眞人]]著神戸新聞総合出版センター</ref>。
== 祭神 == * 六甲比命大善神（[[弁財天]]・吉祥天)=撞賢木厳魂天疎向津姫（[[瀬織津姫]]）
祭神名は六甲の山名の変遷とともに変わってきたようである。水の神としての神徳は六甲周辺において、日本三大名泉の有馬温泉、布引の滝のKOBE WATER、灘五郷の宮水に顕著である。
[[六甲山]]は江戸時代以前には[[武庫]]という漢字があてられ、むこうやま、むこやまと呼ばれ、さらに古くは、[[賀茂真淵]]の『冠辞考』 あまざかるの項に記されているように、向かつ峰の名が伝わるので、祭神名は当初はむかつひめと呼ばれ、近世まではむこうひめとよばれていたと思われる。六甲は「むかつ」と読める。
六甲山は、かつて[[西宮市]][[廣田神社]]の社領であり<ref>『西宮神社』(学生社刊)ｐ５3</ref>、[[廣田神社]]祭神、撞賢木厳魂天疎向津姫（つきさかきいつのみたまあまさかるむかつひめ {{要出典|date=2024年5月|範囲=又の名を瀬織津姫}}）と[[六甲山]]の旧名の向かつ峰の名称が一致する。
== 歴史 == 六甲比命神社の磐座は天然に出来たものではなく[[縄文時代]]のころ、[[縄文人]]たちの手によって巨石を積み上げて出来た人工の磐座である。 一説によれば[[縄文中期]]（BC5,000年頃）に出来たのではないかと言われている。
[[役行者]]の縁者、四鬼家が奈良の[[天川村]]の[[洞川]]より[[唐櫃]]に移住して、西六甲の山を管理していた。
六甲比命講によると、「かつては[[廣田神社]]の奥宮で、さらに[[天照大神|天照坐皇大御神荒御魂]]をお祀りする[[伊勢神宮]]内宮別宮[[荒祭宮]]の奥宮でもあったと考えられる」そうである。ちなみに、荒祭宮は、遷宮制度が導入される以前、内宮正宮の西側敷地で祀られていたことが、伊勢神宮の公式の[https://www.isejingu.or.jp/download/pdf/pamphlet_takihara.pdf 由緒書き]に記されている。また六甲比命講は、六甲比命が向か津姫＝瀬織津姫と同一の神であることを特定し、六甲比命大善神の磐座の存在が六甲山の山名の由来であると推定しているが、これらは『[[ホツマツタエ]]』の記述から導き出されたものであるとする。
瀬織津姫を主祭神としている神社の総本宮と位置づけうる神域とされる。
7世紀、インドからの渡来僧侶[[法道仙人]]が六甲山吉祥院[[多聞寺 (神戸市北区)]]を創建した際、六甲比命神社境内の雲ヶ岩に毘沙門天（多聞天）が降臨したことから、多聞寺の奥の院となった。毘沙門天と吉祥天は仏教本尊の中では珍しく、夫婦の関係である。
その後、江戸時代までは寺院と神社で共同管理されていたが、明治政府の[[神仏分離政策]]によって、境内の雲ヶ岩や法道仙人が作った心経岩は分けて管理されるようになった。
現在は多聞寺と六甲比命講によって共に管理され、秋には六甲比命講と多聞寺住職による護摩供養が催されている。
==近年== 近年、[[パワースポット]]として知られるようになり参拝客も増えている。各種グループの催しも心経岩前で行われることもある。神社拝殿では、由緒書、資料集、関連書籍や、御朱印、六甲比命大善神、六甲瀬織津姫大善神のお札やお守り、水琴鈴、龍や吉祥天の壁掛写真などが授与品として用意されている。
==アクセス== ◎車で：宝塚逆瀬川方面・西宮盤滝・社家郷山方面・芦有道路方面から、あるいは神戸・明石方面から六甲山上道路（[[兵庫県道16号明石神戸宝塚線]]）を通り、六甲山アスレチックパークGREENIA（冬期は六甲山スノーパーク）を目指す。カーナビに 六甲山の上美術館 を入力する。（六甲比命神社専用駐車場、普通車4台分が美術館の10メートル手前にあるため）六甲山スノーパークゲートを通過し、やや路面状況の良くない道路を100メートルほど進む。路面に凹凸があるので十分気を付ける。左手に六甲比命神社の専用駐車場がある。そこが満車の際は、引き返して六甲山アスレチックパークGREENIA（冬期は六甲山スノーパーク）の有料駐車場を利用する。駐車場から徒歩５分程度で参道入口に着く。（トイレは無いので六甲山アスレチックパークGREENIA（冬期は六甲山スノーパーク）のトイレをお借りする必要がある。）
◎公共交通機関で：JR新神戸駅下車。地下鉄に乗車し、三宮で下車。JR三ノ宮駅で東海道線大阪方面行きに乗車し、JR六甲道駅下車、または阪急三宮駅より普通列車で梅田方面へ乗車。阪急六甲駅で下車。16系統のバスに乗車し、六甲ケーブル下で下車。六甲ケーブルに乗って六甲山上駅で下車。山上バスに乗車してアスレチックパーク前のバス停で下車。[https://www.rokkosan.com/top/ticket/?fbclid=IwAR3yru5PE-LQyXb-6QK411OpBe1DVtB4-bUQdD2cb4Iv7-DBCQk3e3S6Ft4 往復の各種チケット]もある。山上道路を100メートルほど引き返して、右側（『北西方向』に上がる道へ進む。300メートルほど進むと突き当りに「六甲比女大神」の看板があり、そこからの細い山道を進んで約3分で仰臥岩、少し引き返して、山道を下って、約1分足らずで、雲ヶ岩。急な坂と仮設階段を下りると1分で六甲比命神社へ到着する。さらに下ると途中右手に巨大な御神体の磐座があり、さらに下って左手に入ると心経岩に至る。引き返して、下りの道を進んで広場に出たところで、右に進めば、六甲山の上美術館、六甲山アスレチックパークGREENIA（冬期は六甲山スノーパーク）方面へ出られる。
== 写真集 == <gallery> |瀬織津姫之大神のお姿を描いた切り絵 ファイル:六甲比命大善神の御神体.JPG|六甲山頂尾根付近に位置する六甲命大善神の磐座 ファイル:六甲比命大善神拝殿 額.JPG|拝殿の額 ファイル:六甲山 心経岩.JPG|心経岩 ファイル:Rokkohime shrine, Kobe 002.jpg|参道（冬季） |六甲山頂駅から六甲比命神社までの案内図 </gallery>
== 出典・注釈 == <references/>
== 関連項目 == * 六甲山吉祥院[[多聞寺 (神戸市北区)]] * [[六甲山]] * [[六甲山神社]] * [[廣田神社]] * [[弁財天]] * [[瀬織津姫]] * [[役行者]] * [[六甲修験]]
== 外部リンク == * [https://rokkouhime.wordpress.com/ 六甲比命神社] * 冠辞考[{{NDLDC|864336}}]

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Rokko Hime Daizen Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= [[File:Rokkohime shrine, Kobe.jpg|280px|The inner shrine of Tamonji, Rokko Hime Shrine]] |location= {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kobe|ja|神戸市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Nada Ward|ja|灘区|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Rokkōyama Town |Latitude degrees = 34 | Latitude minutes = 45 | Latitude seconds = 58.72 |Longitude degrees = 135 | Longitude minutes = 14 | Longitude seconds = 21.91 |Map= Kobe#Japan Hyogo |deity= Rokko Hime Daizen Kami ({{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) |Sacred object= {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座|qq=draft|qq=draft}} |Shrine Rank= |Established= |Main hall= |Other names= |Pilgrimage sites= |festival= |Rituals= }} '''Rokko Hime Daizen Shrine''' (六甲比命大善神社, Rokkō Hime Daizen Jinja) is a Shinto shrine located in Rokkōyama Town, {{ill|Nada Ward|ja|灘区|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kobe|ja|神戸市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. It enshrines an awe-inspiring {{ill|Iwakura (Shinto)|ja|磐座|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (sacred rock formation) as its sacred object.
Situated near the ridge of Mount Rokkō, the shrine is close to another sacred rock associated with {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|天穂日命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, located within the Rokkō Mountain Country House grounds.
The main sacred object, a grand Iwakura known as Yōga Iwa, is nestled between the areas of Un-ga Iwa and Shinkyō Iwa. The shrine serves as the inner sanctuary of {{ill|Tamonji|ja|多聞寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a temple founded in the 7th century by the Indian ascetic {{ill|Hōdō Sennin|ja|法道仙人|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in the Karato district of northern Kobe.<ref>Makoto Tanabe, *Legends of Kobe*, Kobe Shimbun General Publishing Center.</ref>
== deity == * Rokko Hime Daizen Kami ({{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天|qq=draft|qq=draft}}/{{ill|Kichijōten|ja|吉祥天|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), also identified with Tsu Kisakaki Itsu-no-mitama Amasakaru Mukatsu-hime ({{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|qq=draft|qq=draft}}).
The name of the enshrined deity has evolved alongside changes in the naming of Mount Rokkō. As a water deity, the kami's virtues are prominently evident in the region surrounding Mount Rokkō, including Japan's three famous hot springs (Arima Onsen), the renowned Nunobiki Falls (source of Kobe Water), and the legendary Miyamizu water of Nada Gō.
Historically, Mount Rokkō was referred to by the kanji 武庫 (Mukō), pronounced "Mukō-yama" or "Mukō-san," and in earlier times as "Mukatsu-mine," as noted in {{ill|Kamo no Mabuchi|ja|賀茂真淵|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'s *Kanji-kō*. This suggests that the deity was originally known as Mukatsu-hime and later as Mukō-hime before evolving to its current name. The reading "Mukatsu" for "Rokkō" further supports this hypothesis.
Mount Rokkō was once part of the shrine territory of {{ill|Hirota Shrine|ja|廣田神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref>*Nishinomiya Shrine*, Gakuseisha, p. 53.</ref> The deity enshrined at Hirota Shrine, Tsu Kisakaki Itsu-no-mitama Amasakaru Mukatsu-hime (another name for {{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), shares the name with the ancient Mukatsu-mine, aligning the two sites historically and theologically.
== History == The sacred Iwakura of Rokko Hime Shrine is believed to be a man-made structure constructed by stacking massive stones, dating back to the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. It is speculated to have been created around the Middle Jōmon period (circa 3000 BCE).
The shrine was historically associated with the Four Oni family of {{ill|En no Gyōja|ja|役行者|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who managed the western Rokkō mountains after migrating from the {{ill|Dōsen village|ja|洞川|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Tenkawa Village|ja|天川村|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
According to the Rokko Hime Association, the shrine once served as an auxiliary shrine to Hirota Shrine and was also linked to the {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a sub-shrine of the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|lt=inner shrine|ja|皇大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} of {{ill|Ise Jingu|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The association posits that the deity Rokko Hime is synonymous with Seoritsu-hime and attributes the name "Rokkō" to the presence of this sacred site. These interpretations are informed by the ancient texts of {{ill|Hotsuma Tsutae|ja|ホツマツタエ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
The shrine is regarded as the primary sanctuary for worshiping Seoritsu-hime.
In the 7th century, the Indian monk {{ill|Hōdō Sennin|ja|法道仙人|qq=draft|qq=draft}} founded {{ill|Tamonji|ja|多聞寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}} on Mount Rokkō. The Buddhist deity {{ill|Bishamonten|ja|毘沙門天|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Vaiśravaṇa) descended upon the rock formation Un-ga Iwa within the shrine's precincts, establishing its connection to the temple.
During the Edo period, the shrine and the temple were jointly managed. However, with the Meiji government’s {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|ja|神仏分離|qq=draft|qq=draft}} policy, the sacred sites became administratively separated, though they remain closely associated today.
Currently, the shrine is managed jointly by Tamonji and the Rokko Hime Association. Annual autumn rituals include Goma fire offerings conducted by the temple's head priest and the association.
== Recent Developments == In recent years, Rokko Hime Shrine has gained recognition as a {{ill|Power spot|ja|パワースポット|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, attracting numerous visitors. Various group events are held in front of Shinkyō Iwa. The shrine's offerings include detailed pamphlets, Goshuin stamps, talismans dedicated to Rokko Hime Daizen Kami, charms for Seoritsu-hime, musical water bells, and decorative plaques featuring dragons or Kichijōten.
== Access == * By car: Accessible via the Rokko Mountain Roads from Takarazuka, Nishinomiya, or Ashiya. Visitors can aim for the nearby Rokko Mountain Athletic Park or Snow Park, with parking available near the shrine. * By public transport: From JR Shin-Kobe Station, take the subway to Sannomiya, then transfer to JR Tokaido Line for Rokko Station. From there, the Rokko Cable Car and mountain buses provide access to the shrine.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Rokkohime shrine, Kobe 002.jpg|Winter approach to the shrine File:六甲山 心経岩.JPG|Shinkyō Iwa File:六甲比命大善神拝殿 額.JPG|Plaque at the main shrine File:六甲比命大善神の御神体.JPG|Sacred Iwakura of Rokko Hime Daizen Shrine </gallery>

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Rokkō Hime Daizen Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= [[File:Rokkohime shrine, Kobe.jpg|280px|Rokkō Hime Daizen Shrine, Oku-no-in of Taimon-ji Temple]] |location= {{ill|Nada-ku, Kobe|ja|灘区|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Rokkōzan Town|ja|六甲山町|qq=draft|qq=draft}} |Latitude = 34 | Latitude_min = 45 | Latitude_sec = 58.72 |Longitude = 135 | Longitude_min = 14 | Longitude_sec = 21.91 |Map= Kobe#Japan Hyogo |deity= Rokkō Hime Daizen God ({{ill|Saeōrihime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) |Sacred_object= {{ill|Iwakura|ja|磐座|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (sacred rock) |Rank= |consecration_year= |Main_hall= |Other_names= |Special_associations= |Festival= |Rituals= }}
'''Rokkō Hime Daizen Shrine''' (六甲比命大善神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} located in {{ill|Nada-ku, Kobe|ja|灘区|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, known for its sacred {{ill|iwakura rock|ja|磐座|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (rock formation) as the object of worship.
Situated near the ridgeline of {{ill|Mount Rokkō|ja|六甲山|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the shrine is close to other sacred sites such as the rock formation dedicated to {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|天穂日命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} within the Rokkō Country House grounds.
== deity == * Main deity - Rokkō Hime Daizen God (identified as {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Kichijōten|ja|吉祥天|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), also known as Tsukisakaki Itsu-no-Mitama Amasakaru Mukatsu Hime (撞賢木厳魂天疎向津姫, {{ill|Saeōrihime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|qq=draft|qq=draft}}).
The deity’s name has evolved along with the mountain’s name, reflecting its role as a water god influencing the region’s renowned springs such as {{ill|Arima Onsen|ja|有馬温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Nunobiki Waterfall|ja|布引の滝|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and the renowned {{ill|Miya Water|ja|宮水|qq=draft|qq=draft}} used in brewing by the Nada Gōgō.
== History == The shrine’s sacred rock formation is believed to be a man-made structure from the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, possibly constructed around 5,000 BCE. It was used by early Jōmon people for religious practices.
According to legend, the shrine was once the {{ill|Okunomiya|ja|奥宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (inner shrine) of {{ill|Hirota Shrine|ja|廣田神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and was also associated with the {{ill|Ara-matsuri-no-miya|ja|荒祭宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} of the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
The current sacred site is managed collaboratively by {{ill|Taimon-ji Temple|ja|多聞寺 (神戸市北区)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and the {{ill|Rokkō Hime Society|ja|六甲比命講|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, with rituals such as autumn goma fire ceremonies conducted jointly.
== Recent Developments == In recent years, the shrine has gained popularity as a {{ill|power spot|ja|パワースポット|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Various groups now hold events near the sacred rocks, and visitors can obtain shrine-related materials, such as {{ill|goshuin|ja|御朱印|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, talismans, and photos of sacred imagery.
== Access == === By Car === From {{ill|Takarazuka|ja|宝塚|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Nishinomiya|ja|西宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, or {{ill|Kobe|ja|神戸|qq=draft|qq=draft}} via Hyōgo Prefectural Route 16, head toward {{ill|Rokkō Athletic Park GREENIA|ja|六甲山アスレチックパークGREENIA|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Parking is available near the shrine, with additional paid parking at GREENIA.
=== By Public Transportation === From {{ill|JR Shin-Kobe Station|ja|新神戸駅|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, take the subway to {{ill|Sannomiya|ja|三宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Transfer to JR or Hankyū lines to reach {{ill|Rokkō Station|ja|六甲駅|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. From there, take the bus to Rokkō Cable Car Station, ascend to Rokkōzan Station, and proceed to the shrine.
== Photo Gallery == <gallery> File:六甲比命大善神の御神体.JPG|Sacred rock formation of Rokkō Hime Daizen Shrine File:六甲山 心経岩.JPG|Shingyō Rock File:Rokkohime shrine, Kobe 002.jpg|Approach to the shrine (winter) </gallery>
