<!--Rokkō Hime Daizen Shrine--> [[Category:Buildings in Nada-ku, Kobe]] [[Category:History of Nada-ku, Kobe]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kobe]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q17215573}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:六甲比命神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:六甲比命神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:六甲比命神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q17215573]] Wikidata name: Rokkōhime Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:57, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Rokko Hime Daizen Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= File:Mukohime-jinja.jpg |location= [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]], [[:en:Kobe|Kobe]], [[:en:Nada-ku, Kobe|Nada Ward]], Rokkōyama Town |Latitude degrees = 34 | Latitude minutes = 45 | Latitude seconds = 58.72 |Longitude degrees = 135 | Longitude minutes = 14 | Longitude seconds = 21.91 |Map= Kobe#Japan Hyōgo |deity= {{ill|Seoritsuhime|WD=Q13579836}} |Sacred object= [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura (Shinto)]] |Shrine Rank= |Established= |Main hall= |Other names= |Pilgrimage sites= |festival= |Rituals= |prefecture= {{ill|Nada-ku|WD=Q1154555}} |coordinates= {{Coord|34.766306|N|135.239417|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} '''Rokko Hime Daizen Shrine''' (六甲比命大善神社, Rokkō Hime Daizen Jinja) is a Shinto shrine located in Rokkōyama Town, [[:en:Nada-ku, Kobe|Nada Ward]], [[:en:Kobe|Kobe]], [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]]. It enshrines an awe-inspiring [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura (Shinto)]] (sacred rock formation) as its sacred object.
Situated near the ridge of Mount Rokkō, the shrine is close to another sacred rock associated with {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|天穂日命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hohi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アメノホヒ|1=Ame no Hohi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10940685}}, located within the Rokkō Mountain Country House grounds.
The main sacred object, a grand Iwakura known as Yōga Iwa, is nestled between the areas of Un-ga Iwa and Shinkyō Iwa. The shrine serves as the inner sanctuary of {{ill|Tamonji|ja|多聞寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamonji|qq=}}, a temple founded in the 7th century by the Indian ascetic {{ill|Hōdō Sennin|ja|法道仙人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōdō Sennin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 法道|1=Hōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11555373}} in the Karato district of northern Kobe.<ref>Makoto Tanabe, *Legends of Kobe*, Kobe Shimbun General Publishing Center.</ref>
== deity == * Rokko Hime Daizen Kami ({{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Benzaiten|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 弁才天}}/[[:en:Kisshōten|Kichijōten]]), also identified with Tsu Kisakaki Itsu-no-mitama Amasakaru Mukatsu-hime ({{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|qq=}}).
The name of the enshrined deity has evolved alongside changes in the naming of Mount Rokkō. As a water deity, the kami's virtues are prominently evident in the region surrounding Mount Rokkō, including Japan's three famous hot springs (Arima Onsen), the renowned Nunobiki Falls (source of Kobe Water), and the legendary Miyamizu water of Nada Gō.
Historically, Mount Rokkō was referred to by the kanji 武庫 (Mukō), pronounced "Mukō-yama" or "Mukō-san," and in earlier times as "Mukatsu-mine," as noted in [[:en:Kamo no Mabuchi|Kamo no Mabuchi]]'s *Kanji-kō*. This suggests that the deity was originally known as Mukatsu-hime and later as Mukō-hime before evolving to its current name. The reading "Mukatsu" for "Rokkō" further supports this hypothesis.
Mount Rokkō was once part of the shrine territory of [[:en:Hirota Shrine|Hirota Shrine]].<ref>*Nishinomiya Shrine*, Gakuseisha, p. 53.</ref> The deity enshrined at Hirota Shrine, Tsu Kisakaki Itsu-no-mitama Amasakaru Mukatsu-hime (another name for {{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|qq=}}), shares the name with the ancient Mukatsu-mine, aligning the two sites historically and theologically.
== History == The sacred Iwakura of Rokko Hime Shrine is believed to be a man-made structure constructed by stacking massive stones, dating back to the [[:en:Jōmon period|Jōmon period]]. It is speculated to have been created around the Middle Jōmon period (circa 3000 BCE).
The shrine was historically associated with the Four Oni family of {{ill|En no Gyōja|ja|役行者|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/En no Gyōja|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 役小角|1=En no Gyōja|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q198946}}, who managed the western Rokkō mountains after migrating from the {{ill|Dorogawa|ja|洞川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dōsen village|qq=}} in [[:en:Tenkawa, Nara|Tenkawa Village]].
According to the Rokko Hime Association, the shrine once served as an auxiliary shrine to Hirota Shrine and was also linked to the {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aramatsuri-no-Miya|qq=}}, a sub-shrine of the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|lt=inner shrine|ja|皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Jingu]]. The association posits that the deity Rokko Hime is synonymous with Seoritsu-hime and attributes the name "Rokkō" to the presence of this sacred site. These interpretations are informed by the ancient texts of {{ill|Hotsuma Tsutae|ja|ホツマツタエ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotsuma Tsutae|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ホツマツタヱ}}.
The shrine is regarded as the primary sanctuary for worshiping Seoritsu-hime.
In the 7th century, the Indian monk {{ill|Hōdō Sennin|ja|法道仙人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōdō Sennin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 法道|1=Hōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11555373}} founded {{ill|Tamonji|ja|多聞寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamonji|qq=}} on Mount Rokkō. The Buddhist deity [[:en:Vaiśravaṇa|Bishamonten]] (Vaiśravaṇa) descended upon the rock formation Un-ga Iwa within the shrine's precincts, establishing its connection to the temple.
During the Edo period, the shrine and the temple were jointly managed. However, with the Meiji government’s [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] policy, the sacred sites became administratively separated, though they remain closely associated today.
Currently, the shrine is managed jointly by Tamonji and the Rokko Hime Association. Annual autumn rituals include Goma fire offerings conducted by the temple's head priest and the association.
== Recent Developments == In recent years, Rokko Hime Shrine has gained recognition as a [[:en:Power spot (spirituality)|Power spot]], attracting numerous visitors. Various group events are held in front of Shinkyō Iwa. The shrine's offerings include detailed pamphlets, Goshuin stamps, talismans dedicated to Rokko Hime Daizen Kami, charms for Seoritsu-hime, musical water bells, and decorative plaques featuring dragons or Kichijōten.
== Access == * By car: Accessible via the Rokko Mountain Roads from Takarazuka, Nishinomiya, or Ashiya. Visitors can aim for the nearby Rokko Mountain Athletic Park or Snow Park, with parking available near the shrine. * By public transport: From JR Shin-Kobe Station, take the subway to Sannomiya, then transfer to JR Tokaido Line for Rokko Station. From there, the Rokko Cable Car and mountain buses provide access to the shrine.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Rokkohime shrine, Kobe 002.jpg|Winter approach to the shrine File:六甲山 心経岩.JPG|Shinkyō Iwa File:六甲比命大善神拝殿 額.JPG|Plaque at the main shrine File:六甲比命大善神の御神体.JPG|Sacred Iwakura of Rokko Hime Daizen Shrine </gallery>

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Rokkō Hime Daizen Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= [[File:Rokkohime shrine, Kobe.jpg|280px|Rokkō Hime Daizen Shrine, Oku-no-in of Taimon-ji Temple]] |location= [[:en:Nada-ku, Kobe|Nada-ku, Kobe]], [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]], {{ill|Rokkōzan Town|ja|六甲山町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokkōzan Town|1=Rokkōsanchō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11392467}} |Latitude = 34 | Latitude_min = 45 | Latitude_sec = 58.72 |Longitude = 135 | Longitude_min = 14 | Longitude_sec = 21.91 |Map= Kobe#Japan Hyōgo |deity= Rokkō Hime Daizen God ({{ill|Saeōrihime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saeōrihime|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|qq=}}) |Sacred_object= [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura]] (sacred rock) |Rank= |consecration_year= |Main_hall= |Other_names= |Special_associations= |Festival= |Rituals= }}
'''Rokkō Hime Daizen Shrine''' (六甲比命大善神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Nada-ku, Kobe|Nada-ku, Kobe]], [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]], known for its sacred [[:en:Iwakura rock|iwakura rock]] (rock formation) as the object of worship.
Situated near the ridgeline of [[:en:Mount Rokkō|Mount Rokkō]], the shrine is close to other sacred sites such as the rock formation dedicated to {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|天穂日命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hohi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アメノホヒ|1=Ame no Hohi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10940685}} within the Rokkō Country House grounds.
== deity == * Main deity - Rokkō Hime Daizen God (identified as {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Benzaiten|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 弁才天}} and [[:en:Kisshōten|Kichijōten]]), also known as Tsukisakaki Itsu-no-Mitama Amasakaru Mukatsu Hime (撞賢木厳魂天疎向津姫, {{ill|Saeōrihime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saeōrihime|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|qq=}}).
The deity’s name has evolved along with the mountain’s name, reflecting its role as a water god influencing the region’s renowned springs such as [[:en:Arima Onsen|Arima Onsen]], {{ill|Nunobiki Waterfall|ja|布引の滝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nunobiki Waterfall|1=Nunobiki Waterfall|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q45344962}}, and the renowned {{ill|Miya Water|ja|宮水|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miya Water|qq=}} used in brewing by the Nada Gōgō.
== History == The shrine’s sacred rock formation is believed to be a man-made structure from the [[:en:Jōmon period|Jōmon period]], possibly constructed around 5,000 BCE. It was used by early Jōmon people for religious practices.
According to legend, the shrine was once the {{ill|Okumiya|lt=Okumiya|lt=Okunomiya|ja|奥宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okunomiya|qq=}} (inner shrine) of [[:en:Hirota Shrine|Hirota Shrine]] and was also associated with the {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ara-matsuri-no-miya|qq=}} of the [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]].
The current sacred site is managed collaboratively by {{ill|Taimon-ji Temple|ja|多聞寺 (神戸市北区)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taimon-ji Temple|qq=}} and the {{ill|Rokkō Hime Society|ja|六甲比命講|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokkō Hime Society|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, with rituals such as autumn goma fire ceremonies conducted jointly.
== Recent Developments == In recent years, the shrine has gained popularity as a [[:en:Power spot (spirituality)|power spot]]. Various groups now hold events near the sacred rocks, and visitors can obtain shrine-related materials, such as {{ill|Shuin|lt=Shuin|lt=Goshuin|ja|御朱印|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/goshuin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 朱印 (神社仏閣)}}, talismans, and photos of sacred imagery.
== Access == === By Car === From [[:en:Takarazuka|Takarazuka]], {{ill|Nishinomiya|ja|西宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishinomiya|1=Nishinomiya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15890060}}, or {{ill|Kobe|ja|神戸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobe|1=Kobe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11589706}} via Hyōgo Prefectural Route 16, head toward {{ill|Rokkō Athletic Park GREENIA|ja|六甲山アスレチックパークGREENIA|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokkō Athletic Park GREENIA|1=Category:Mount Rokko Adventure Kingdom GREENIA|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q134464307}}. Parking is available near the shrine, with additional paid parking at GREENIA.
=== By Public Transportation === From [[:en:Shin-Kōbe Station|JR Shin-Kobe Station]], take the subway to [[:en:Sannomiya|Sannomiya]]. Transfer to JR or Hankyū lines to reach [[:en:Rokkō Station|Rokkō Station]]. From there, take the bus to Rokkō Cable Car Station, ascend to Rokkōzan Station, and proceed to the shrine.

It enshrines an awe-inspiring [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura (Shinto)]] (sacred rock formation) as its sacred object.
Situated near the ridge of Mount Rokkō, the shrine is close to another sacred rock associated with {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|天穂日命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hohi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アメノホヒ|1=Ame no Hohi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10940685}}, located within the Rokkō Mountain Country House grounds.
The main sacred object, a grand Iwakura known as Yōga Iwa, is nestled between the areas of Un-ga Iwa and Shinkyō Iwa. The shrine serves as the inner sanctuary of {{ill|Tamonji|ja|多聞寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamonji|qq=}}, a temple founded in the 7th century by the Indian ascetic {{ill|Hōdō Sennin|ja|法道仙人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōdō Sennin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 法道|1=Hōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11555373}} in the Karato district of northern Kobe.<ref>Makoto Tanabe, *Legends of Kobe*, Kobe Shimbun General Publishing Center.</ref>
== deity == * Rokko Hime Daizen Kami ({{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Benzaiten|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 弁才天}}/[[:en:Kisshōten|Kichijōten]]), also identified with Tsu Kisakaki Itsu-no-mitama Amasakaru Mukatsu-hime ({{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|qq=}}).
The name of the enshrined deity has evolved alongside changes in the naming of Mount Rokkō. As a water deity, the kami's virtues are prominently evident in the region surrounding Mount Rokkō, including Japan's three famous hot springs (Arima Onsen), the renowned Nunobiki Falls (source of Kobe Water), and the legendary Miyamizu water of Nada Gō.
Historically, Mount Rokkō was referred to by the kanji 武庫 (Mukō), pronounced "Mukō-yama" or "Mukō-san," and in earlier times as "Mukatsu-mine," as noted in [[:en:Kamo no Mabuchi|Kamo no Mabuchi]]'s *Kanji-kō*. This suggests that the deity was originally known as Mukatsu-hime and later as Mukō-hime before evolving to its current name. The reading "Mukatsu" for "Rokkō" further supports this hypothesis.
Mount Rokkō was once part of the shrine territory of [[:en:Hirota Shrine|Hirota Shrine]].<ref>*Nishinomiya Shrine*, Gakuseisha, p. 53.</ref> The deity enshrined at Hirota Shrine, Tsu Kisakaki Itsu-no-mitama Amasakaru Mukatsu-hime (another name for {{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|qq=}}), shares the name with the ancient Mukatsu-mine, aligning the two sites historically and theologically.
== History == The sacred Iwakura of Rokko Hime Shrine is believed to be a man-made structure constructed by stacking massive stones, dating back to the [[:en:Jōmon period|Jōmon period]]. It is speculated to have been created around the Middle Jōmon period (circa 3000 BCE).
The shrine was historically associated with the Four Oni family of {{ill|En no Gyōja|ja|役行者|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/En no Gyōja|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 役小角|1=En no Gyōja|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q198946}}, who managed the western Rokkō mountains after migrating from the {{ill|Dorogawa|ja|洞川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dōsen village|qq=}} in [[:en:Tenkawa, Nara|Tenkawa Village]].
According to the Rokko Hime Association, the shrine once served as an auxiliary shrine to Hirota Shrine and was also linked to the {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aramatsuri-no-Miya|qq=}}, a sub-shrine of the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|lt=inner shrine|ja|皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Jingu]]. The association posits that the deity Rokko Hime is synonymous with Seoritsu-hime and attributes the name "Rokkō" to the presence of this sacred site. These interpretations are informed by the ancient texts of {{ill|Hotsuma Tsutae|ja|ホツマツタエ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotsuma Tsutae|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ホツマツタヱ}}.
The shrine is regarded as the primary sanctuary for worshiping Seoritsu-hime.
In the 7th century, the Indian monk {{ill|Hōdō Sennin|ja|法道仙人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōdō Sennin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 法道|1=Hōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11555373}} founded {{ill|Tamonji|ja|多聞寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamonji|qq=}} on Mount Rokkō. The Buddhist deity [[:en:Vaiśravaṇa|Bishamonten]] (Vaiśravaṇa) descended upon the rock formation Un-ga Iwa within the shrine's precincts, establishing its connection to the temple.
During the Edo period, the shrine and the temple were jointly managed. However, with the Meiji government’s [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] policy, the sacred sites became administratively separated, though they remain closely associated today.
Currently, the shrine is managed jointly by Tamonji and the Rokko Hime Association. Annual autumn rituals include Goma fire offerings conducted by the temple's head priest and the association.
== Recent Developments == In recent years, Rokko Hime Shrine has gained recognition as a [[:en:Power spot (spirituality)|Power spot]], attracting numerous visitors. Various group events are held in front of Shinkyō Iwa. The shrine's offerings include detailed pamphlets, Goshuin stamps, talismans dedicated to Rokko Hime Daizen Kami, charms for Seoritsu-hime, musical water bells, and decorative plaques featuring dragons or Kichijōten.
== Access == * By car: Accessible via the Rokko Mountain Roads from Takarazuka, Nishinomiya, or Ashiya. Visitors can aim for the nearby Rokko Mountain Athletic Park or Snow Park, with parking available near the shrine. * By public transport: From JR Shin-Kobe Station, take the subway to Sannomiya, then transfer to JR Tokaido Line for Rokko Station. From there, the Rokko Cable Car and mountain buses provide access to the shrine.

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Rokkō Hime Daizen Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= [[File:Rokkohime shrine, Kobe.jpg|280px|Rokkō Hime Daizen Shrine, Oku-no-in of Taimon-ji Temple]] |location= [[:en:Nada-ku, Kobe|Nada-ku, Kobe]], [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]], {{ill|Rokkōzan Town|ja|六甲山町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokkōzan Town|1=Rokkōsanchō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11392467}} |Latitude = 34 | Latitude_min = 45 | Latitude_sec = 58.72 |Longitude = 135 | Longitude_min = 14 | Longitude_sec = 21.91 |Map= Kobe#Japan Hyōgo |deity= Rokkō Hime Daizen God ({{ill|Saeōrihime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saeōrihime|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|qq=}}) |Sacred_object= [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura]] (sacred rock) |Rank= |consecration_year= |Main_hall= |Other_names= |Special_associations= |Festival= |Rituals= }}
'''Rokkō Hime Daizen Shrine''' (六甲比命大善神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Nada-ku, Kobe|Nada-ku, Kobe]], [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]], known for its sacred [[:en:Iwakura rock|iwakura rock]] (rock formation) as the object of worship.
Situated near the ridgeline of [[:en:Mount Rokkō|Mount Rokkō]], the shrine is close to other sacred sites such as the rock formation dedicated to {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|天穂日命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hohi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アメノホヒ|1=Ame no Hohi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10940685}} within the Rokkō Country House grounds.
== deity == * Main deity - Rokkō Hime Daizen God (identified as {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Benzaiten|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 弁才天}} and [[:en:Kisshōten|Kichijōten]]), also known as Tsukisakaki Itsu-no-Mitama Amasakaru Mukatsu Hime (撞賢木厳魂天疎向津姫, {{ill|Saeōrihime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saeōrihime|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|qq=}}).
The deity’s name has evolved along with the mountain’s name, reflecting its role as a water god influencing the region’s renowned springs such as [[:en:Arima Onsen|Arima Onsen]], {{ill|Nunobiki Waterfall|ja|布引の滝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nunobiki Waterfall|1=Nunobiki Waterfall|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q45344962}}, and the renowned {{ill|Miya Water|ja|宮水|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miya Water|qq=}} used in brewing by the Nada Gōgō.
== History == The shrine’s sacred rock formation is believed to be a man-made structure from the [[:en:Jōmon period|Jōmon period]], possibly constructed around 5,000 BCE. It was used by early Jōmon people for religious practices.
According to legend, the shrine was once the {{ill|Okumiya|lt=Okumiya|lt=Okunomiya|ja|奥宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okunomiya|qq=}} (inner shrine) of [[:en:Hirota Shrine|Hirota Shrine]] and was also associated with the {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ara-matsuri-no-miya|qq=}} of the [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]].
The current sacred site is managed collaboratively by {{ill|Taimon-ji Temple|ja|多聞寺 (神戸市北区)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taimon-ji Temple|qq=}} and the {{ill|Rokkō Hime Society|ja|六甲比命講|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokkō Hime Society|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, with rituals such as autumn goma fire ceremonies conducted jointly.
== Recent Developments == In recent years, the shrine has gained popularity as a [[:en:Power spot (spirituality)|power spot]]. Various groups now hold events near the sacred rocks, and visitors can obtain shrine-related materials, such as {{ill|Shuin|lt=Shuin|lt=Goshuin|ja|御朱印|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/goshuin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 朱印 (神社仏閣)}}, talismans, and photos of sacred imagery.
== Access == === By Car === From [[:en:Takarazuka|Takarazuka]], {{ill|Nishinomiya|ja|西宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishinomiya|1=Nishinomiya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15890060}}, or {{ill|Kobe|ja|神戸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobe|1=Kobe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11589706}} via Hyōgo Prefectural Route 16, head toward {{ill|Rokkō Athletic Park GREENIA|ja|六甲山アスレチックパークGREENIA|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokkō Athletic Park GREENIA|1=Category:Mount Rokko Adventure Kingdom GREENIA|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q134464307}}. Parking is available near the shrine, with additional paid parking at GREENIA.
=== By Public Transportation === From [[:en:Shin-Kōbe Station|JR Shin-Kobe Station]], take the subway to [[:en:Sannomiya|Sannomiya]]. Transfer to JR or Hankyū lines to reach [[:en:Rokkō Station|Rokkō Station]]. From there, take the bus to Rokkō Cable Car Station, ascend to Rokkōzan Station, and proceed to the shrine.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:六甲比命神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:六甲比命神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:六甲比命神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q17215573]] Wikidata name: Rokkōhime Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:57, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Rokko Hime Daizen Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= File:Mukohime-jinja.jpg |location= [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]], [[:en:Kobe|Kobe]], [[:en:Nada-ku, Kobe|Nada Ward]], Rokkōyama Town |Latitude degrees = 34 | Latitude minutes = 45 | Latitude seconds = 58.72 |Longitude degrees = 135 | Longitude minutes = 14 | Longitude seconds = 21.91 |Map= Kobe#Japan Hyōgo |deity= {{ill|Seoritsuhime|WD=Q13579836}} |Sacred object= [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura (Shinto)]] |Shrine Rank= |Established= |Main hall= |Other names= |Pilgrimage sites= |festival= |Rituals= |prefecture= {{ill|Nada-ku|WD=Q1154555}} |coordinates= {{Coord|34.766306|N|135.239417|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} '''Rokko Hime Daizen Shrine''' (六甲比命大善神社, Rokkō Hime Daizen Jinja) is a Shinto shrine located in Rokkōyama Town, [[:en:Nada-ku, Kobe|Nada Ward]], [[:en:Kobe|Kobe]], [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]]. It enshrines an awe-inspiring [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura (Shinto)]] (sacred rock formation) as its sacred object.
Situated near the ridge of Mount Rokkō, the shrine is close to another sacred rock associated with {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|天穂日命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hohi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アメノホヒ|1=Ame no Hohi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10940685}}, located within the Rokkō Mountain Country House grounds.
The main sacred object, a grand Iwakura known as Yōga Iwa, is nestled between the areas of Un-ga Iwa and Shinkyō Iwa. The shrine serves as the inner sanctuary of {{ill|Tamonji|ja|多聞寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamonji|qq=}}, a temple founded in the 7th century by the Indian ascetic {{ill|Hōdō Sennin|ja|法道仙人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōdō Sennin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 法道|1=Hōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11555373}} in the Karato district of northern Kobe.<ref>Makoto Tanabe, *Legends of Kobe*, Kobe Shimbun General Publishing Center.</ref>
== deity == * Rokko Hime Daizen Kami ({{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Benzaiten|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 弁才天}}/[[:en:Kisshōten|Kichijōten]]), also identified with Tsu Kisakaki Itsu-no-mitama Amasakaru Mukatsu-hime ({{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|qq=}}).
The name of the enshrined deity has evolved alongside changes in the naming of Mount Rokkō. As a water deity, the kami's virtues are prominently evident in the region surrounding Mount Rokkō, including Japan's three famous hot springs (Arima Onsen), the renowned Nunobiki Falls (source of Kobe Water), and the legendary Miyamizu water of Nada Gō.

The shrine is regarded as the primary sanctuary for worshiping Seoritsu-hime.
In the 7th century, the Indian monk {{ill|Hōdō Sennin|ja|法道仙人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōdō Sennin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 法道|1=Hōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11555373}} founded {{ill|Tamonji|ja|多聞寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamonji|qq=}} on Mount Rokkō. Currently, the shrine is managed jointly by Tamonji and the Rokko Hime Association. Annual autumn rituals include Goma fire offerings conducted by the temple's head priest and the association.

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Rokkō Hime Daizen Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= [[File:Rokkohime shrine, Kobe.jpg|280px|Rokkō Hime Daizen Shrine, Oku-no-in of Taimon-ji Temple]] |location= [[:en:Nada-ku, Kobe|Nada-ku, Kobe]], [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]], {{ill|Rokkōzan Town|ja|六甲山町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokkōzan Town|1=Rokkōsanchō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11392467}} |Latitude = 34 | Latitude_min = 45 | Latitude_sec = 58.72 |Longitude = 135 | Longitude_min = 14 | Longitude_sec = 21.91 |Map= Kobe#Japan Hyōgo |deity= Rokkō Hime Daizen God ({{ill|Saeōrihime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saeōrihime|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|qq=}}) |Sacred_object= [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura]] (sacred rock) |Rank= |consecration_year= |Main_hall= |Other_names= |Special_associations= |Festival= |Rituals= }}
'''Rokkō Hime Daizen Shrine''' (六甲比命大善神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Nada-ku, Kobe|Nada-ku, Kobe]], [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]], known for its sacred [[:en:Iwakura rock|iwakura rock]] (rock formation) as the object of worship.
Situated near the ridgeline of [[:en:Mount Rokkō|Mount Rokkō]], the shrine is close to other sacred sites such as the rock formation dedicated to {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|天穂日命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hohi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アメノホヒ|1=Ame no Hohi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10940685}} within the Rokkō Country House grounds.
== deity == * Main deity - Rokkō Hime Daizen God (identified as {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Benzaiten|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 弁才天}} and [[:en:Kisshōten|Kichijōten]]), also known as Tsukisakaki Itsu-no-Mitama Amasakaru Mukatsu Hime (撞賢木厳魂天疎向津姫, {{ill|Saeōrihime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saeōrihime|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|qq=}}).
The deity’s name has evolved along with the mountain’s name, reflecting its role as a water god influencing the region’s renowned springs such as [[:en:Arima Onsen|Arima Onsen]], {{ill|Nunobiki Waterfall|ja|布引の滝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nunobiki Waterfall|1=Nunobiki Waterfall|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q45344962}}, and the renowned {{ill|Miya Water|ja|宮水|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miya Water|qq=}} used in brewing by the Nada Gōgō.
== History == The shrine’s sacred rock formation is believed to be a man-made structure from the [[:en:Jōmon period|Jōmon period]], possibly constructed around 5,000 BCE. It was used by early Jōmon people for religious practices.
According to legend, the shrine was once the {{ill|Okumiya|lt=Okumiya|lt=Okunomiya|ja|奥宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okunomiya|qq=}} (inner shrine) of [[:en:Hirota Shrine|Hirota Shrine]] and was also associated with the {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ara-matsuri-no-miya|qq=}} of the [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]].
The current sacred site is managed collaboratively by {{ill|Taimon-ji Temple|ja|多聞寺 (神戸市北区)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taimon-ji Temple|qq=}} and the {{ill|Rokkō Hime Society|ja|六甲比命講|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokkō Hime Society|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, with rituals such as autumn goma fire ceremonies conducted jointly.
== Recent Developments == In recent years, the shrine has gained popularity as a [[:en:Power spot (spirituality)|power spot]]. Various groups now hold events near the sacred rocks, and visitors can obtain shrine-related materials, such as {{ill|Shuin|lt=Shuin|lt=Goshuin|ja|御朱印|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/goshuin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 朱印 (神社仏閣)}}, talismans, and photos of sacred imagery.
== Access == === By Car === From [[:en:Takarazuka|Takarazuka]], {{ill|Nishinomiya|ja|西宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishinomiya|1=Nishinomiya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15890060}}, or {{ill|Kobe|ja|神戸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobe|1=Kobe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11589706}} via Hyōgo Prefectural Route 16, head toward {{ill|Rokkō Athletic Park GREENIA|ja|六甲山アスレチックパークGREENIA|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokkō Athletic Park GREENIA|1=Category:Mount Rokko Adventure Kingdom GREENIA|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q134464307}}. Parking is available near the shrine, with additional paid parking at GREENIA.
=== By Public Transportation === From [[:en:Shin-Kōbe Station|JR Shin-Kobe Station]], take the subway to [[:en:Sannomiya|Sannomiya]]. Transfer to JR or Hankyū lines to reach [[:en:Rokkō Station|Rokkō Station]]. From there, take the bus to Rokkō Cable Car Station, ascend to Rokkōzan Station, and proceed to the shrine.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:六甲比命神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:六甲比命神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:六甲比命神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q17215573]] Wikidata name: Rokkōhime Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:57, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Rokko Hime Daizen Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= File:Mukohime-jinja.jpg |location= [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]], [[:en:Kobe|Kobe]], [[:en:Nada-ku, Kobe|Nada Ward]], Rokkōyama Town |Latitude degrees = 34 | Latitude minutes = 45 | Latitude seconds = 58.72 |Longitude degrees = 135 | Longitude minutes = 14 | Longitude seconds = 21.91 |Map= Kobe#Japan Hyōgo |deity= {{ill|Seoritsuhime|WD=Q13579836}} |Sacred object= [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura (Shinto)]] |Shrine Rank= |Established= |Main hall= |Other names= |Pilgrimage sites= |festival= |Rituals= |prefecture= {{ill|Nada-ku|WD=Q1154555}} |coordinates= {{Coord|34.766306|N|135.239417|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} '''Rokko Hime Daizen Shrine''' (六甲比命大善神社, Rokkō Hime Daizen Jinja) is a Shinto shrine located in Rokkōyama Town, [[:en:Nada-ku, Kobe|Nada Ward]], [[:en:Kobe|Kobe]], [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo Prefecture]]. It enshrines an awe-inspiring [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura (Shinto)]] (sacred rock formation) as its sacred object.
Situated near the ridge of Mount Rokkō, the shrine is close to another sacred rock associated with {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|天穂日命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hohi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アメノホヒ|1=Ame no Hohi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10940685}}, located within the Rokkō Mountain Country House grounds.
The main sacred object, a grand Iwakura known as Yōga Iwa, is nestled between the areas of Un-ga Iwa and Shinkyō Iwa. The shrine serves as the inner sanctuary of {{ill|Tamonji|ja|多聞寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamonji|qq=}}, a temple founded in the 7th century by the Indian ascetic {{ill|Hōdō Sennin|ja|法道仙人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōdō Sennin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 法道|1=Hōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11555373}} in the Karato district of northern Kobe.<ref>Makoto Tanabe, *Legends of Kobe*, Kobe Shimbun General Publishing Center.</ref>
== deity == * Rokko Hime Daizen Kami ({{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Benzaiten|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 弁才天}}/[[:en:Kisshōten|Kichijōten]]), also identified with Tsu Kisakaki Itsu-no-mitama Amasakaru Mukatsu-hime ({{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|qq=}}).
The name of the enshrined deity has evolved alongside changes in the naming of Mount Rokkō. As a water deity, the kami's virtues are prominently evident in the region surrounding Mount Rokkō, including Japan's three famous hot springs (Arima Onsen), the renowned Nunobiki Falls (source of Kobe Water), and the legendary Miyamizu water of Nada Gō.
Historically, Mount Rokkō was referred to by the kanji 武庫 (Mukō), pronounced "Mukō-yama" or "Mukō-san," and in earlier times as "Mukatsu-mine," as noted in [[:en:Kamo no Mabuchi|Kamo no Mabuchi]]'s *Kanji-kō*. This suggests that the deity was originally known as Mukatsu-hime and later as Mukō-hime before evolving to its current name. The reading "Mukatsu" for "Rokkō" further supports this hypothesis.
Mount Rokkō was once part of the shrine territory of [[:en:Hirota Shrine|Hirota Shrine]].<ref>*Nishinomiya Shrine*, Gakuseisha, p. 53.</ref> The deity enshrined at Hirota Shrine, Tsu Kisakaki Itsu-no-mitama Amasakaru Mukatsu-hime (another name for {{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|qq=}}), shares the name with the ancient Mukatsu-mine, aligning the two sites historically and theologically.
== History == The sacred Iwakura of Rokko Hime Shrine is believed to be a man-made structure constructed by stacking massive stones, dating back to the [[:en:Jōmon period|Jōmon period]]. It is speculated to have been created around the Middle Jōmon period (circa 3000 BCE).
The shrine was historically associated with the Four Oni family of {{ill|En no Gyōja|ja|役行者|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/En no Gyōja|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 役小角|1=En no Gyōja|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q198946}}, who managed the western Rokkō mountains after migrating from the {{ill|Dorogawa|ja|洞川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dōsen village|qq=}} in [[:en:Tenkawa, Nara|Tenkawa Village]].
According to the Rokko Hime Association, the shrine once served as an auxiliary shrine to Hirota Shrine and was also linked to the {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aramatsuri-no-Miya|qq=}}, a sub-shrine of the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|lt=inner shrine|ja|皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Jingu]]. The association posits that the deity Rokko Hime is synonymous with Seoritsu-hime and attributes the name "Rokkō" to the presence of this sacred site. These interpretations are informed by the ancient texts of {{ill|Hotsuma Tsutae|ja|ホツマツタエ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotsuma Tsutae|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ホツマツタヱ}}.
The shrine is regarded as the primary sanctuary for worshiping Seoritsu-hime.
In the 7th century, the Indian monk {{ill|Hōdō Sennin|ja|法道仙人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōdō Sennin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 法道|1=Hōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11555373}} founded {{ill|Tamonji|ja|多聞寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamonji|qq=}} on Mount Rokkō. The Buddhist deity [[:en:Vaiśravaṇa|Bishamonten]] (Vaiśravaṇa) descended upon the rock formation Un-ga Iwa within the shrine's precincts, establishing its connection to the temple.
During the Edo period, the shrine and the temple were jointly managed. However, with the Meiji government’s [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] policy, the sacred sites became administratively separated, though they remain closely associated today.
Currently, the shrine is managed jointly by Tamonji and the Rokko Hime Association. Annual autumn rituals include Goma fire offerings conducted by the temple's head priest and the association.
== Recent Developments == In recent years, Rokko Hime Shrine has gained recognition as a [[:en:Power spot (spirituality)|Power spot]], attracting numerous visitors. Various group events are held in front of Shinkyō Iwa. The shrine's offerings include detailed pamphlets, Goshuin stamps, talismans dedicated to Rokko Hime Daizen Kami, charms for Seoritsu-hime, musical water bells, and decorative plaques featuring dragons or Kichijōten.
== Access == * By car: Accessible via the Rokko Mountain Roads from Takarazuka, Nishinomiya, or Ashiya. Visitors can aim for the nearby Rokko Mountain Athletic Park or Snow Park, with parking available near the shrine. * By public transport: From JR Shin-Kobe Station, take the subway to Sannomiya, then transfer to JR Tokaido Line for Rokko Station. From there, the Rokko Cable Car and mountain buses provide access to the shrine.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:六甲比命神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:六甲比命神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:六甲比命神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q17215573]] Wikidata name: Rokkōhime Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:57, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Rokko Hime Daizen Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= File:Mukohime-jinja.jpg |location= {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture}}, {{ill|Kobe|en|Kobe|WD=Q48320|lt=Kobe}}, {{ill|Nada-ku, Kobe|en|Nada-ku, Kobe|WD=Q1154555|lt=Nada Ward}}, Rokkōyama Town |Latitude degrees = 34 | Latitude minutes = 45 | Latitude seconds = 58.72 |Longitude degrees = 135 | Longitude minutes = 14 | Longitude seconds = 21.91 |Map= Kobe#Japan Hyōgo |deity= {{ill|Seoritsuhime|WD=Q13579836}} |Sacred object= {{ill|Iwakura rock|en|Iwakura rock|WD=Q11587880|lt=Iwakura (Shinto)}} |Shrine Rank= |Established= |Main hall= |Other names= |Pilgrimage sites= |festival= |Rituals= |prefecture= {{ill|Nada-ku|WD=Q1154555}} |coordinates= {{Coord|34.766306|N|135.239417|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} '''Rokko Hime Daizen Shrine''' (六甲比命大善神社, Rokkō Hime Daizen Jinja) is a Shinto shrine located in Rokkōyama Town, {{ill|Nada-ku, Kobe|en|Nada-ku, Kobe|WD=Q1154555|lt=Nada Ward}}, {{ill|Kobe|en|Kobe|WD=Q48320|lt=Kobe}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture}}. It enshrines an awe-inspiring {{ill|Iwakura rock|en|Iwakura rock|WD=Q11587880|lt=Iwakura (Shinto)}} (sacred rock formation) as its sacred object.
Situated near the ridge of Mount Rokkō, the shrine is close to another sacred rock associated with {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|天穂日命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hohi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アメノホヒ|1=Ame no Hohi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10940685}}, located within the Rokkō Mountain Country House grounds.
The main sacred object, a grand Iwakura known as Yōga Iwa, is nestled between the areas of Un-ga Iwa and Shinkyō Iwa. The shrine serves as the inner sanctuary of {{ill|Tamonji|ja|多聞寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamonji|qq=}}, a temple founded in the 7th century by the Indian ascetic {{ill|Hōdō Sennin|ja|法道仙人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōdō Sennin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 法道|1=Hōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11555373}} in the Karato district of northern Kobe.<ref>Makoto Tanabe, *Legends of Kobe*, Kobe Shimbun General Publishing Center.</ref>
== deity == * Rokko Hime Daizen Kami ({{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Benzaiten|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 弁才天}}/[[:en:Kisshōten|Kichijōten]]), also identified with Tsu Kisakaki Itsu-no-mitama Amasakaru Mukatsu-hime ({{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|qq=}}).
The name of the enshrined deity has evolved alongside changes in the naming of Mount Rokkō. As a water deity, the kami's virtues are prominently evident in the region surrounding Mount Rokkō, including Japan's three famous hot springs (Arima Onsen), the renowned Nunobiki Falls (source of Kobe Water), and the legendary Miyamizu water of Nada Gō.
Historically, Mount Rokkō was referred to by the kanji 武庫 (Mukō), pronounced "Mukō-yama" or "Mukō-san," and in earlier times as "Mukatsu-mine," as noted in {{ill|Kamo no Mabuchi|en|Kamo no Mabuchi|WD=Q1343397|lt=Kamo no Mabuchi}}'s *Kanji-kō*. This suggests that the deity was originally known as Mukatsu-hime and later as Mukō-hime before evolving to its current name. The reading "Mukatsu" for "Rokkō" further supports this hypothesis.
Mount Rokkō was once part of the shrine territory of {{ill|Hirota Shrine|en|Hirota Shrine|WD=Q1466105|lt=Hirota Shrine}}.<ref>*Nishinomiya Shrine*, Gakuseisha, p. 53.</ref> The deity enshrined at Hirota Shrine, Tsu Kisakaki Itsu-no-mitama Amasakaru Mukatsu-hime (another name for {{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|qq=}}), shares the name with the ancient Mukatsu-mine, aligning the two sites historically and theologically.
== History == The sacred Iwakura of Rokko Hime Shrine is believed to be a man-made structure constructed by stacking massive stones, dating back to the {{ill|Jōmon period|en|Jōmon period|WD=Q52824|lt=Jōmon period}}. It is speculated to have been created around the Middle Jōmon period (circa 3000 BCE).
The shrine was historically associated with the Four Oni family of {{ill|En no Gyōja|ja|役行者|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/En no Gyōja|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 役小角|1=En no Gyōja|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q198946}}, who managed the western Rokkō mountains after migrating from the {{ill|Dorogawa|ja|洞川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dōsen village|qq=}} in {{ill|Tenkawa, Nara|en|Tenkawa, Nara|WD=Q1203501|lt=Tenkawa Village}}.
According to the Rokko Hime Association, the shrine once served as an auxiliary shrine to Hirota Shrine and was also linked to the {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aramatsuri-no-Miya|qq=}}, a sub-shrine of the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|lt=inner shrine|ja|皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} of {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Jingu}}. The association posits that the deity Rokko Hime is synonymous with Seoritsu-hime and attributes the name "Rokkō" to the presence of this sacred site. These interpretations are informed by the ancient texts of {{ill|Hotsuma Tsutae|ja|ホツマツタエ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotsuma Tsutae|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ホツマツタヱ}}.
The shrine is regarded as the primary sanctuary for worshiping Seoritsu-hime.
In the 7th century, the Indian monk {{ill|Hōdō Sennin|ja|法道仙人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōdō Sennin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 法道|1=Hōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11555373}} founded {{ill|Tamonji|ja|多聞寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamonji|qq=}} on Mount Rokkō. The Buddhist deity {{ill|Vaiśravaṇa|en|Vaiśravaṇa|WD=Q866315|lt=Bishamonten}} (Vaiśravaṇa) descended upon the rock formation Un-ga Iwa within the shrine's precincts, establishing its connection to the temple.
During the Edo period, the shrine and the temple were jointly managed. However, with the Meiji government’s {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri}} policy, the sacred sites became administratively separated, though they remain closely associated today.
Currently, the shrine is managed jointly by Tamonji and the Rokko Hime Association. Annual autumn rituals include Goma fire offerings conducted by the temple's head priest and the association.
== Recent Developments == In recent years, Rokko Hime Shrine has gained recognition as a {{ill|Power spot (spirituality)|en|Power spot (spirituality)|WD=Q11329568|lt=Power spot}}, attracting numerous visitors. Various group events are held in front of Shinkyō Iwa. The shrine's offerings include detailed pamphlets, Goshuin stamps, talismans dedicated to Rokko Hime Daizen Kami, charms for Seoritsu-hime, musical water bells, and decorative plaques featuring dragons or Kichijōten.
== Access == * By car: Accessible via the Rokko Mountain Roads from Takarazuka, Nishinomiya, or Ashiya. Visitors can aim for the nearby Rokko Mountain Athletic Park or Snow Park, with parking available near the shrine. * By public transport: From JR Shin-Kobe Station, take the subway to Sannomiya, then transfer to JR Tokaido Line for Rokko Station. From there, the Rokko Cable Car and mountain buses provide access to the shrine.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Rokkohime shrine, Kobe 002.jpg|Winter approach to the shrine File:六甲山 心経岩.JPG|Shinkyō Iwa File:六甲比命大善神拝殿 額.JPG|Plaque at the main shrine File:六甲比命大善神の御神体.JPG|Sacred Iwakura of Rokko Hime Daizen Shrine </gallery>
== References == <references/>
== See also == * {{ill|Tamonji|ja|多聞寺 (神戸市北区)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamonji|qq=}} * {{ill|Mount Rokkō|en|Mount Rokkō|WD=Q462120|lt=Mount Rokko}} * {{ill|Hirota Shrine|en|Hirota Shrine|WD=Q1466105|lt=Hirota Shrine}} * {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Benzaiten|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 弁才天}} * {{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|qq=}} * {{ill|En no Gyōja|ja|役行者|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/En no Gyōja|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 役小角|1=En no Gyōja|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q198946}} * {{ill|Rokkō Shugen|ja|六甲修験|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokkō Shugen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ==Merge==
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Rokkō Hime Daizen Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= [[File:Rokkohime shrine, Kobe.jpg|280px|Rokkō Hime Daizen Shrine, Oku-no-in of Taimon-ji Temple]] |location= {{ill|Nada-ku, Kobe|en|Nada-ku, Kobe|WD=Q1154555|lt=Nada-ku, Kobe}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture}}, {{ill|Rokkōzan Town|ja|六甲山町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokkōzan Town|1=Rokkōsanchō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11392467}} |Latitude = 34 | Latitude_min = 45 | Latitude_sec = 58.72 |Longitude = 135 | Longitude_min = 14 | Longitude_sec = 21.91 |Map= Kobe#Japan Hyōgo |deity= Rokkō Hime Daizen God ({{ill|Saeōrihime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saeōrihime|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|qq=}}) |Sacred_object= {{ill|Iwakura rock|en|Iwakura rock|WD=Q11587880|lt=Iwakura}} (sacred rock) |Rank= |consecration_year= |Main_hall= |Other_names= |Special_associations= |Festival= |Rituals= }}
'''Rokkō Hime Daizen Shrine''' (六甲比命大善神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Nada-ku, Kobe|en|Nada-ku, Kobe|WD=Q1154555|lt=Nada-ku, Kobe}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture}}, known for its sacred {{ill|Iwakura rock|en|Iwakura rock|WD=Q11587880|lt=iwakura rock}} (rock formation) as the object of worship.
Situated near the ridgeline of {{ill|Mount Rokkō|en|Mount Rokkō|WD=Q462120|lt=Mount Rokkō}}, the shrine is close to other sacred sites such as the rock formation dedicated to {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|天穂日命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hohi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アメノホヒ|1=Ame no Hohi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10940685}} within the Rokkō Country House grounds.
== deity == * Main deity - Rokkō Hime Daizen God (identified as {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Benzaiten|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 弁才天}} and [[:en:Kisshōten|Kichijōten]]), also known as Tsukisakaki Itsu-no-Mitama Amasakaru Mukatsu Hime (撞賢木厳魂天疎向津姫, {{ill|Saeōrihime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saeōrihime|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|qq=}}).
The deity’s name has evolved along with the mountain’s name, reflecting its role as a water god influencing the region’s renowned springs such as {{ill|Arima Onsen|en|Arima Onsen|WD=Q4790673|lt=Arima Onsen}}, {{ill|Nunobiki Waterfall|ja|布引の滝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nunobiki Waterfall|1=Nunobiki Waterfall|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q45344962}}, and the renowned {{ill|Miya Water|ja|宮水|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miya Water|qq=}} used in brewing by the Nada Gōgō.
== History == The shrine’s sacred rock formation is believed to be a man-made structure from the {{ill|Jōmon period|en|Jōmon period|WD=Q52824|lt=Jōmon period}}, possibly constructed around 5,000 BCE. It was used by early Jōmon people for religious practices.
According to legend, the shrine was once the {{ill|Okumiya|lt=Okumiya|lt=Okunomiya|ja|奥宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okunomiya|qq=}} (inner shrine) of {{ill|Hirota Shrine|en|Hirota Shrine|WD=Q1466105|lt=Hirota Shrine}} and was also associated with the {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ara-matsuri-no-miya|qq=}} of the {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine}}.
The current sacred site is managed collaboratively by {{ill|Taimon-ji Temple|ja|多聞寺 (神戸市北区)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taimon-ji Temple|qq=}} and the {{ill|Rokkō Hime Society|ja|六甲比命講|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokkō Hime Society|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, with rituals such as autumn goma fire ceremonies conducted jointly.
== Recent Developments == In recent years, the shrine has gained popularity as a {{ill|Power spot (spirituality)|en|Power spot (spirituality)|WD=Q11329568|lt=power spot}}. Various groups now hold events near the sacred rocks, and visitors can obtain shrine-related materials, such as {{ill|Shuin|lt=Shuin|lt=Goshuin|ja|御朱印|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/goshuin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 朱印 (神社仏閣)}}, talismans, and photos of sacred imagery.
== Access == === By Car === From {{ill|Takarazuka|en|Takarazuka|WD=Q7677266|lt=Takarazuka}}, {{ill|Nishinomiya|ja|西宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishinomiya|1=Nishinomiya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15890060}}, or {{ill|Kobe|ja|神戸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobe|1=Kobe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11589706}} via Hyōgo Prefectural Route 16, head toward {{ill|Rokkō Athletic Park GREENIA|ja|六甲山アスレチックパークGREENIA|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokkō Athletic Park GREENIA|1=Category:Mount Rokko Adventure Kingdom GREENIA|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q134464307}}. Parking is available near the shrine, with additional paid parking at GREENIA.
=== By Public Transportation === From {{ill|Shin-Kōbe Station|en|Shin-Kōbe Station|WD=Q801435|lt=JR Shin-Kobe Station}}, take the subway to {{ill|Sannomiya|en|Sannomiya|WD=Q7418864|lt=Sannomiya}}. Transfer to JR or Hankyū lines to reach {{ill|Rokkō Station|en|Rokkō Station|WD=Q3970702|lt=Rokkō Station}}. From there, take the bus to Rokkō Cable Car Station, ascend to Rokkōzan Station, and proceed to the shrine.
== Photo Gallery == <gallery> File:六甲比命大善神の御神体.JPG|Sacred rock formation of Rokkō Hime Daizen Shrine File:六甲山 心経岩.JPG|Shingyō Rock File:Rokkohime shrine, Kobe 002.jpg|Approach to the shrine (winter) </gallery>

This suggests that the deity was originally known as Mukatsu-hime and later as Mukō-hime before evolving to its current name. The reading "Mukatsu" for "Rokkō" further supports this hypothesis.
Mount Rokkō was once part of the shrine territory of {{ill|Hirota Shrine|en|Hirota Shrine|WD=Q1466105|lt=Hirota Shrine}}.<ref>*Nishinomiya Shrine*, Gakuseisha, p. 53.</ref> The deity enshrined at Hirota Shrine, Tsu Kisakaki Itsu-no-mitama Amasakaru Mukatsu-hime (another name for {{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|qq=}}), shares the name with the ancient Mukatsu-mine, aligning the two sites historically and theologically.
== History == The sacred Iwakura of Rokko Hime Shrine is believed to be a man-made structure constructed by stacking massive stones, dating back to the {{ill|Jōmon period|en|Jōmon period|WD=Q52824|lt=Jōmon period}}. It is speculated to have been created around the Middle Jōmon period (circa 3000 BCE).
The shrine was historically associated with the Four Oni family of {{ill|En no Gyōja|ja|役行者|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/En no Gyōja|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 役小角|1=En no Gyōja|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q198946}}, who managed the western Rokkō mountains after migrating from the {{ill|Dorogawa|ja|洞川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dōsen village|qq=}} in {{ill|Tenkawa, Nara|en|Tenkawa, Nara|WD=Q1203501|lt=Tenkawa Village}}.
According to the Rokko Hime Association, the shrine once served as an auxiliary shrine to Hirota Shrine and was also linked to the {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aramatsuri-no-Miya|qq=}}, a sub-shrine of the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|lt=inner shrine|ja|皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} of {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Jingu}}. The association posits that the deity Rokko Hime is synonymous with Seoritsu-hime and attributes the name "Rokkō" to the presence of this sacred site. These interpretations are informed by the ancient texts of {{ill|Hotsuma Tsutae|ja|ホツマツタエ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotsuma Tsutae|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ホツマツタヱ}}.
The shrine is regarded as the primary sanctuary for worshiping Seoritsu-hime.
In the 7th century, the Indian monk {{ill|Hōdō Sennin|ja|法道仙人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōdō Sennin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 法道|1=Hōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11555373}} founded {{ill|Tamonji|ja|多聞寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamonji|qq=}} on Mount Rokkō. The Buddhist deity {{ill|Vaiśravaṇa|en|Vaiśravaṇa|WD=Q866315|lt=Bishamonten}} (Vaiśravaṇa) descended upon the rock formation Un-ga Iwa within the shrine's precincts, establishing its connection to the temple.
During the Edo period, the shrine and the temple were jointly managed. However, with the Meiji government’s {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri}} policy, the sacred sites became administratively separated, though they remain closely associated today.
Currently, the shrine is managed jointly by Tamonji and the Rokko Hime Association. Annual autumn rituals include Goma fire offerings conducted by the temple's head priest and the association.
== Recent Developments == In recent years, Rokko Hime Shrine has gained recognition as a {{ill|Power spot (spirituality)|en|Power spot (spirituality)|WD=Q11329568|lt=Power spot}}, attracting numerous visitors. Various group events are held in front of Shinkyō Iwa. The shrine's offerings include detailed pamphlets, Goshuin stamps, talismans dedicated to Rokko Hime Daizen Kami, charms for Seoritsu-hime, musical water bells, and decorative plaques featuring dragons or Kichijōten.
== Access == * By car: Accessible via the Rokko Mountain Roads from Takarazuka, Nishinomiya, or Ashiya. Visitors can aim for the nearby Rokko Mountain Athletic Park or Snow Park, with parking available near the shrine. * By public transport: From JR Shin-Kobe Station, take the subway to Sannomiya, then transfer to JR Tokaido Line for Rokko Station. From there, the Rokko Cable Car and mountain buses provide access to the shrine.

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Rokkō Hime Daizen Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= [[File:Rokkohime shrine, Kobe.jpg|280px|Rokkō Hime Daizen Shrine, Oku-no-in of Taimon-ji Temple]] |location= {{ill|Nada-ku, Kobe|en|Nada-ku, Kobe|WD=Q1154555|lt=Nada-ku, Kobe}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture}}, {{ill|Rokkōzan Town|ja|六甲山町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokkōzan Town|1=Rokkōsanchō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11392467}} |Latitude = 34 | Latitude_min = 45 | Latitude_sec = 58.72 |Longitude = 135 | Longitude_min = 14 | Longitude_sec = 21.91 |Map= Kobe#Japan Hyōgo |deity= Rokkō Hime Daizen God ({{ill|Saeōrihime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saeōrihime|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|qq=}}) |Sacred_object= {{ill|Iwakura rock|en|Iwakura rock|WD=Q11587880|lt=Iwakura}} (sacred rock) |Rank= |consecration_year= |Main_hall= |Other_names= |Special_associations= |Festival= |Rituals= }}
'''Rokkō Hime Daizen Shrine''' (六甲比命大善神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Nada-ku, Kobe|en|Nada-ku, Kobe|WD=Q1154555|lt=Nada-ku, Kobe}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture}}, known for its sacred {{ill|Iwakura rock|en|Iwakura rock|WD=Q11587880|lt=iwakura rock}} (rock formation) as the object of worship.
Situated near the ridgeline of {{ill|Mount Rokkō|en|Mount Rokkō|WD=Q462120|lt=Mount Rokkō}}, the shrine is close to other sacred sites such as the rock formation dedicated to {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|天穂日命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hohi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アメノホヒ|1=Ame no Hohi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10940685}} within the Rokkō Country House grounds.
== deity == * Main deity - Rokkō Hime Daizen God (identified as {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Benzaiten|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 弁才天}} and [[:en:Kisshōten|Kichijōten]]), also known as Tsukisakaki Itsu-no-Mitama Amasakaru Mukatsu Hime (撞賢木厳魂天疎向津姫, {{ill|Saeōrihime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saeōrihime|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|qq=}}).
The deity’s name has evolved along with the mountain’s name, reflecting its role as a water god influencing the region’s renowned springs such as {{ill|Arima Onsen|en|Arima Onsen|WD=Q4790673|lt=Arima Onsen}}, {{ill|Nunobiki Waterfall|ja|布引の滝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nunobiki Waterfall|1=Nunobiki Waterfall|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q45344962}}, and the renowned {{ill|Miya Water|ja|宮水|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miya Water|qq=}} used in brewing by the Nada Gōgō.
== History == The shrine’s sacred rock formation is believed to be a man-made structure from the {{ill|Jōmon period|en|Jōmon period|WD=Q52824|lt=Jōmon period}}, possibly constructed around 5,000 BCE. It was used by early Jōmon people for religious practices.
According to legend, the shrine was once the {{ill|Okumiya|lt=Okumiya|lt=Okunomiya|ja|奥宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okunomiya|qq=}} (inner shrine) of {{ill|Hirota Shrine|en|Hirota Shrine|WD=Q1466105|lt=Hirota Shrine}} and was also associated with the {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ara-matsuri-no-miya|qq=}} of the {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine}}.
The current sacred site is managed collaboratively by {{ill|Taimon-ji Temple|ja|多聞寺 (神戸市北区)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taimon-ji Temple|qq=}} and the {{ill|Rokkō Hime Society|ja|六甲比命講|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokkō Hime Society|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, with rituals such as autumn goma fire ceremonies conducted jointly.
== Recent Developments == In recent years, the shrine has gained popularity as a {{ill|Power spot (spirituality)|en|Power spot (spirituality)|WD=Q11329568|lt=power spot}}. Various groups now hold events near the sacred rocks, and visitors can obtain shrine-related materials, such as {{ill|Shuin|lt=Shuin|lt=Goshuin|ja|御朱印|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/goshuin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 朱印 (神社仏閣)}}, talismans, and photos of sacred imagery.
== Access == === By Car === From {{ill|Takarazuka|en|Takarazuka|WD=Q7677266|lt=Takarazuka}}, {{ill|Nishinomiya|ja|西宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishinomiya|1=Nishinomiya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15890060}}, or {{ill|Kobe|ja|神戸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobe|1=Kobe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11589706}} via Hyōgo Prefectural Route 16, head toward {{ill|Rokkō Athletic Park GREENIA|ja|六甲山アスレチックパークGREENIA|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokkō Athletic Park GREENIA|1=Category:Mount Rokko Adventure Kingdom GREENIA|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q134464307}}. Parking is available near the shrine, with additional paid parking at GREENIA.
=== By Public Transportation === From {{ill|Shin-Kōbe Station|en|Shin-Kōbe Station|WD=Q801435|lt=JR Shin-Kobe Station}}, take the subway to {{ill|Sannomiya|en|Sannomiya|WD=Q7418864|lt=Sannomiya}}. Transfer to JR or Hankyū lines to reach {{ill|Rokkō Station|en|Rokkō Station|WD=Q3970702|lt=Rokkō Station}}. From there, take the bus to Rokkō Cable Car Station, ascend to Rokkōzan Station, and proceed to the shrine.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:六甲比命神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:六甲比命神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:六甲比命神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q17215573]] Wikidata name: Rokkōhime Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:57, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Rokko Hime Daizen Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= File:Mukohime-jinja.jpg |location= {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture}}, {{ill|Kobe|en|Kobe|WD=Q48320|lt=Kobe}}, {{ill|Nada-ku, Kobe|en|Nada-ku, Kobe|WD=Q1154555|lt=Nada Ward}}, Rokkōyama Town |Latitude degrees = 34 | Latitude minutes = 45 | Latitude seconds = 58.72 |Longitude degrees = 135 | Longitude minutes = 14 | Longitude seconds = 21.91 |Map= Kobe#Japan Hyōgo |deity= {{ill|Seoritsuhime|WD=Q13579836}} |Sacred object= {{ill|Iwakura rock|en|Iwakura rock|WD=Q11587880|lt=Iwakura (Shinto)}} |Shrine Rank= |Established= |Main hall= |Other names= |Pilgrimage sites= |festival= |Rituals= |prefecture= {{ill|Nada-ku|WD=Q1154555}} |coordinates= {{Coord|34.766306|N|135.239417|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} '''Rokko Hime Daizen Shrine''' (六甲比命大善神社, Rokkō Hime Daizen Jinja) is a Shinto shrine located in Rokkōyama Town, {{ill|Nada-ku, Kobe|en|Nada-ku, Kobe|WD=Q1154555|lt=Nada Ward}}, {{ill|Kobe|en|Kobe|WD=Q48320|lt=Kobe}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture}}. It enshrines an awe-inspiring {{ill|Iwakura rock|en|Iwakura rock|WD=Q11587880|lt=Iwakura (Shinto)}} (sacred rock formation) as its sacred object.
Situated near the ridge of Mount Rokkō, the shrine is close to another sacred rock associated with {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|天穂日命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hohi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アメノホヒ|1=Ame no Hohi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10940685}}, located within the Rokkō Mountain Country House grounds.
The main sacred object, a grand Iwakura known as Yōga Iwa, is nestled between the areas of Un-ga Iwa and Shinkyō Iwa. The shrine serves as the inner sanctuary of {{ill|Tamonji|ja|多聞寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamonji|qq=}}, a temple founded in the 7th century by the Indian ascetic {{ill|Hōdō Sennin|ja|法道仙人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōdō Sennin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 法道|1=Hōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11555373}} in the Karato district of northern Kobe.<ref>Makoto Tanabe, *Legends of Kobe*, Kobe Shimbun General Publishing Center.</ref>
== deity == * Rokko Hime Daizen Kami ({{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Benzaiten|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 弁才天}}/[[:en:Kisshōten|Kichijōten]]), also identified with Tsu Kisakaki Itsu-no-mitama Amasakaru Mukatsu-hime ({{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|qq=}}).
The name of the enshrined deity has evolved alongside changes in the naming of Mount Rokkō. As a water deity, the kami's virtues are prominently evident in the region surrounding Mount Rokkō, including Japan's three famous hot springs (Arima Onsen), the renowned Nunobiki Falls (source of Kobe Water), and the legendary Miyamizu water of Nada Gō.
Historically, Mount Rokkō was referred to by the kanji 武庫 (Mukō), pronounced "Mukō-yama" or "Mukō-san," and in earlier times as "Mukatsu-mine," as noted in {{ill|Kamo no Mabuchi|en|Kamo no Mabuchi|WD=Q1343397|lt=Kamo no Mabuchi}}'s *Kanji-kō*. This suggests that the deity was originally known as Mukatsu-hime and later as Mukō-hime before evolving to its current name. The reading "Mukatsu" for "Rokkō" further supports this hypothesis.
Mount Rokkō was once part of the shrine territory of {{ill|Hirota Shrine|en|Hirota Shrine|WD=Q1466105|lt=Hirota Shrine}}.<ref>*Nishinomiya Shrine*, Gakuseisha, p. 53.</ref> The deity enshrined at Hirota Shrine, Tsu Kisakaki Itsu-no-mitama Amasakaru Mukatsu-hime (another name for {{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|qq=}}), shares the name with the ancient Mukatsu-mine, aligning the two sites historically and theologically.

It is speculated to have been created around the Middle Jōmon period (circa 3000 BCE).
The shrine was historically associated with the Four Oni family of {{ill|En no Gyōja|ja|役行者|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/En no Gyōja|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 役小角|1=En no Gyōja|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q198946}}, who managed the western Rokkō mountains after migrating from the {{ill|Dorogawa|ja|洞川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dōsen village|qq=}} in {{ill|Tenkawa, Nara|en|Tenkawa, Nara|WD=Q1203501|lt=Tenkawa Village}}.
According to the Rokko Hime Association, the shrine once served as an auxiliary shrine to Hirota Shrine and was also linked to the {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aramatsuri-no-Miya|qq=}}, a sub-shrine of the {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|lt=inner shrine|ja|皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} of {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Jingu}}. The association posits that the deity Rokko Hime is synonymous with Seoritsu-hime and attributes the name "Rokkō" to the presence of this sacred site. These interpretations are informed by the ancient texts of {{ill|Hotsuma Tsutae|ja|ホツマツタエ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotsuma Tsutae|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ホツマツタヱ}}.
The shrine is regarded as the primary sanctuary for worshiping Seoritsu-hime.
In the 7th century, the Indian monk {{ill|Hōdō Sennin|ja|法道仙人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōdō Sennin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 法道|1=Hōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11555373}} founded {{ill|Tamonji|ja|多聞寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamonji|qq=}} on Mount Rokkō. The Buddhist deity {{ill|Vaiśravaṇa|en|Vaiśravaṇa|WD=Q866315|lt=Bishamonten}} (Vaiśravaṇa) descended upon the rock formation Un-ga Iwa within the shrine's precincts, establishing its connection to the temple.
During the Edo period, the shrine and the temple were jointly managed. However, with the Meiji government’s {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri}} policy, the sacred sites became administratively separated, though they remain closely associated today.
Currently, the shrine is managed jointly by Tamonji and the Rokko Hime Association. Annual autumn rituals include Goma fire offerings conducted by the temple's head priest and the association.
== Recent Developments == In recent years, Rokko Hime Shrine has gained recognition as a {{ill|Power spot (spirituality)|en|Power spot (spirituality)|WD=Q11329568|lt=Power spot}}, attracting numerous visitors. Various group events are held in front of Shinkyō Iwa. The shrine's offerings include detailed pamphlets, Goshuin stamps, talismans dedicated to Rokko Hime Daizen Kami, charms for Seoritsu-hime, musical water bells, and decorative plaques featuring dragons or Kichijōten.
== Access == * By car: Accessible via the Rokko Mountain Roads from Takarazuka, Nishinomiya, or Ashiya. Visitors can aim for the nearby Rokko Mountain Athletic Park or Snow Park, with parking available near the shrine. * By public transport: From JR Shin-Kobe Station, take the subway to Sannomiya, then transfer to JR Tokaido Line for Rokko Station. From there, the Rokko Cable Car and mountain buses provide access to the shrine.

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name= Rokkō Hime Daizen Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= [[File:Rokkohime shrine, Kobe.jpg|280px|Rokkō Hime Daizen Shrine, Oku-no-in of Taimon-ji Temple]] |location= {{ill|Nada-ku, Kobe|en|Nada-ku, Kobe|WD=Q1154555|lt=Nada-ku, Kobe}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture}}, {{ill|Rokkōzan Town|ja|六甲山町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokkōzan Town|1=Rokkōsanchō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11392467}} |Latitude = 34 | Latitude_min = 45 | Latitude_sec = 58.72 |Longitude = 135 | Longitude_min = 14 | Longitude_sec = 21.91 |Map= Kobe#Japan Hyōgo |deity= Rokkō Hime Daizen God ({{ill|Saeōrihime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saeōrihime|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|qq=}}) |Sacred_object= {{ill|Iwakura rock|en|Iwakura rock|WD=Q11587880|lt=Iwakura}} (sacred rock) |Rank= |consecration_year= |Main_hall= |Other_names= |Special_associations= |Festival= |Rituals= }}
'''Rokkō Hime Daizen Shrine''' (六甲比命大善神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Nada-ku, Kobe|en|Nada-ku, Kobe|WD=Q1154555|lt=Nada-ku, Kobe}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture}}, known for its sacred {{ill|Iwakura rock|en|Iwakura rock|WD=Q11587880|lt=iwakura rock}} (rock formation) as the object of worship.
Situated near the ridgeline of {{ill|Mount Rokkō|en|Mount Rokkō|WD=Q462120|lt=Mount Rokkō}}, the shrine is close to other sacred sites such as the rock formation dedicated to {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi|ja|天穂日命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hohi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アメノホヒ|1=Ame no Hohi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10940685}} within the Rokkō Country House grounds.
== deity == * Main deity - Rokkō Hime Daizen God (identified as {{ill|Benzaiten|ja|弁財天|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Benzaiten|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 弁才天}} and [[:en:Kisshōten|Kichijōten]]), also known as Tsukisakaki Itsu-no-Mitama Amasakaru Mukatsu Hime (撞賢木厳魂天疎向津姫, {{ill|Saeōrihime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saeōrihime|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|qq=}}).
The deity’s name has evolved along with the mountain’s name, reflecting its role as a water god influencing the region’s renowned springs such as {{ill|Arima Onsen|en|Arima Onsen|WD=Q4790673|lt=Arima Onsen}}, {{ill|Nunobiki Waterfall|ja|布引の滝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nunobiki Waterfall|1=Nunobiki Waterfall|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q45344962}}, and the renowned {{ill|Miya Water|ja|宮水|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miya Water|qq=}} used in brewing by the Nada Gōgō.
== History == The shrine’s sacred rock formation is believed to be a man-made structure from the {{ill|Jōmon period|en|Jōmon period|WD=Q52824|lt=Jōmon period}}, possibly constructed around 5,000 BCE. It was used by early Jōmon people for religious practices.
According to legend, the shrine was once the {{ill|Okumiya|lt=Okumiya|lt=Okunomiya|ja|奥宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okunomiya|qq=}} (inner shrine) of {{ill|Hirota Shrine|en|Hirota Shrine|WD=Q1466105|lt=Hirota Shrine}} and was also associated with the {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ara-matsuri-no-miya|qq=}} of the {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine}}.
The current sacred site is managed collaboratively by {{ill|Taimon-ji Temple|ja|多聞寺 (神戸市北区)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taimon-ji Temple|qq=}} and the {{ill|Rokkō Hime Society|ja|六甲比命講|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokkō Hime Society|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, with rituals such as autumn goma fire ceremonies conducted jointly.
== Recent Developments == In recent years, the shrine has gained popularity as a {{ill|Power spot (spirituality)|en|Power spot (spirituality)|WD=Q11329568|lt=power spot}}. Various groups now hold events near the sacred rocks, and visitors can obtain shrine-related materials, such as {{ill|Shuin|lt=Shuin|lt=Goshuin|ja|御朱印|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/goshuin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 朱印 (神社仏閣)}}, talismans, and photos of sacred imagery.
== Access == === By Car === From {{ill|Takarazuka|en|Takarazuka|WD=Q7677266|lt=Takarazuka}}, {{ill|Nishinomiya|ja|西宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishinomiya|1=Nishinomiya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15890060}}, or {{ill|Kobe|ja|神戸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobe|1=Kobe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11589706}} via Hyōgo Prefectural Route 16, head toward {{ill|Rokkō Athletic Park GREENIA|ja|六甲山アスレチックパークGREENIA|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokkō Athletic Park GREENIA|1=Category:Mount Rokko Adventure Kingdom GREENIA|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q134464307}}. Parking is available near the shrine, with additional paid parking at GREENIA.
=== By Public Transportation === From {{ill|Shin-Kōbe Station|en|Shin-Kōbe Station|WD=Q801435|lt=JR Shin-Kobe Station}}, take the subway to {{ill|Sannomiya|en|Sannomiya|WD=Q7418864|lt=Sannomiya}}. Transfer to JR or Hankyū lines to reach {{ill|Rokkō Station|en|Rokkō Station|WD=Q3970702|lt=Rokkō Station}}. From there, take the bus to Rokkō Cable Car Station, ascend to Rokkōzan Station, and proceed to the shrine.
== Photo Gallery == <gallery> File:六甲比命大善神の御神体.JPG|Sacred rock formation of Rokkō Hime Daizen Shrine File:六甲山 心経岩.JPG|Shingyō Rock File:Rokkohime shrine, Kobe 002.jpg|Approach to the shrine (winter) </gallery>
